items.prompt: Plain `sort` puts '10' before '9'. Which flag fixes that so numbers sort by value?
choices: -r
-n
-u
-f
items.explanation: -n sorts numerically, so 9 comes before 10. Default sort is alphabetical, where '10' sorts before '9' because it compares the first character '1' against '9'. -r only reverses the order.

items.prompt: What does `sort -rn` give you?
choices: Alphabetical order, A to Z
Numeric order, smallest first
Numeric order, largest first
Random order
items.explanation: -n makes the sort numeric and -r reverses it, so together you get largest-number-first. This is the usual finishing step for a 'top counts' list.

items.prompt: Why do you usually run `sort` before `uniq -c`?
choices: sort makes uniq run faster but is optional
uniq only compares neighbouring lines, so identical lines must first be grouped together
uniq cannot read files directly
sort removes the header line
items.explanation: uniq only collapses or counts lines that are adjacent. If duplicates are scattered through the file, sort must first bring them next to each other for uniq -c to tally them correctly.

items.prompt: What does the -c flag add to uniq?
choices: It makes the comparison case-sensitive
It prints a count of how many times each line repeated
It clears duplicate files
It compresses the output
items.explanation: uniq -c prefixes each line with the number of times it occurred in the (adjacent) run. That count is what turns a sorted list into a frequency tally.

items.prompt: You want to know how many lines are in access.log. Which command?
choices: wc -w access.log
wc -l access.log
wc -c access.log
head access.log
items.explanation: wc -l counts lines. -w counts words and -c counts bytes/characters, neither of which is the line count. head would just show the first few lines.

items.prompt: Which command shows the LAST 20 lines of a file?
choices: head -n 20 file
tail -n 20 file
cut -n 20 file
sort -n 20 file
items.explanation: tail shows the end of a file, and -n 20 asks for 20 lines. head shows the beginning instead; cut and sort do not take a line-count like this.

items.prompt: What is special about `tail -f server.log`?
choices: It shows the first lines only
It keeps running and prints new lines as they are added to the file
It finds a pattern in the file
It deletes the file after reading
items.explanation: -f means 'follow': tail stays open and streams new lines as the file grows, which is ideal for watching a live log. It does not stop after printing the current end.

items.prompt: What does `tr 'A-Z' 'a-z' < file` do?
choices: Deletes all capital letters
Translates every uppercase letter to its lowercase form
Finds lines with capital letters
Sorts the file by case
items.explanation: tr maps each character in the first set to the matching character in the second set, so A->a, B->b, and so on, lowercasing the text. To delete characters you would use tr -d instead.

lessons.title: Building Pipelines (and jq for JSON)
lessons.content:
The real magic of these tools is **combining** them with pipes. Each tool does one small job and hands its output to the next, so a single line can answer a question about a huge file. The trick is to build the pipeline **one stage at a time** and look at the output after each `|`.

The most famous idiom is the **top-N count**. Suppose a web server log has the visitor's IP address as the first column, and you want the busiest visitors:

```bash
awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
```

Read it left to right:
1. `awk '{print $1}'` pulls out just the IP column.
2. `sort` groups identical IPs together (so uniq can count them).
3. `uniq -c` counts how many times each IP appears.
4. `sort -rn` orders those counts largest-first.
5. `head` keeps just the top few.

Mix in grep to filter first. To find the IPs causing the most 'not found' (404) errors:

```bash
grep " 404 " access.log | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
```

That is grep (keep only 404 lines) -> awk (take the IP) -> the same counting tail. Each stage is simple; together they are powerful.

**jq: pipelines for JSON.** A lot of modern tools output **JSON**, a structured format with braces and quotes rather than plain columns. grep and awk are clumsy with JSON because the data spans nested structures. `jq` is a small tool made just for querying JSON: you give it a path to the data you want.

```bash
jq '.name' person.json              # the value of the "name" key
jq -r '.users[].email' data.json    # every user's email, one per line
```

The `.name` is a path into the JSON. `.users[]` means 'each item in the users list'. The `-r` flag prints **raw** text without the surrounding quotes, which is what you want when feeding the result into another command.

**Recap:** pipe simple tools together to answer big questions; the `sort | uniq -c | sort -rn` tail is the classic 'count and rank' pattern. For JSON, reach for `jq` with a path like `.users[].email`, and add `-r` for unquoted output.

items.prompt: What is the core idea of a pipeline like `a | b | c`?
choices: It runs a, b, and c as three unrelated commands
Each command's output becomes the next command's input
It saves a, b, and c into one file
It runs the commands in reverse order
items.explanation: A pipe connects programs end to end: the output of a flows into b, and b's output flows into c. This lets you build a big result from small single-purpose tools.

items.prompt: What is the recommended way to build a long pipeline you are unsure about?
choices: Type the whole thing and hope it works
Add one stage at a time and check the output after each |
Always start from the last command
Avoid pipes and use one huge awk program
items.explanation: Building incrementally, checking the output after each | , lets you catch a mistake at the exact stage it happens instead of debugging the whole chain at once.

items.prompt: In `... | sort | uniq -c | ...`, why is sort placed before uniq -c?
choices: To make the output numeric
To bring identical lines together so uniq -c can count them
To reverse the order
Because uniq cannot read piped input otherwise
items.explanation: uniq -c only counts runs of adjacent identical lines, so sort must first group matching lines next to each other. This pairing is the heart of the count-and-rank idiom.

items.prompt: In the idiom `sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head`, what is the job of the SECOND sort (`sort -rn`)?
choices: To group identical lines for counting
To order the counts so the largest is at the top
To remove duplicate counts
To convert text to numbers
items.explanation: After uniq -c each line begins with a count. sort -rn orders those counts numerically and in reverse, so the biggest counts rise to the top, ready for head to show the winners.

items.prompt: Given the pipeline `awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head`, what does the final output look like?
choices: The first 10 lines of the raw log
A list of counts paired with IP addresses, busiest first
Every unique IP in alphabetical order
The total number of requests as a single number
items.explanation: awk extracts the IP column, uniq -c attaches a count to each unique IP, and sort -rn | head leaves the highest counts on top. So you get lines like '57 10.0.0.5', ranked from most to least frequent.

items.prompt: You only want to count 404 errors per IP. Where should grep " 404 " go in the pipeline?
choices: At the very end, after head
First, so later stages only see the 404 lines
It does not matter where grep goes
Replace awk with grep entirely
items.explanation: Filtering first with grep means every later stage works on the smaller, relevant set of 404 lines. Putting grep at the end would filter the already-counted summary, which is not what you want.

items.prompt: Why use jq instead of grep/awk for JSON data?
choices: jq is faster at sorting numbers
jq understands JSON's nested structure, so you can ask for data by its path
jq replaces text in place like sed
grep cannot read files but jq can
items.explanation: JSON is nested (objects inside arrays inside objects), which trips up line-oriented tools like grep and awk. jq parses the structure so you can pull out a value by path, like .users[].email.

items.prompt: What does the -r flag do in `jq -r '.name' file.json`?
choices: Reads the file recursively from subfolders
Reverses the JSON array
Prints the string value raw, without the surrounding double quotes
Restricts output to the first match
items.explanation: -r means 'raw output': string values print without JSON's double quotes, which is what you want when feeding the value into another command. Without -r, a name would print as "alice" with quotes.

items.prompt: In jq, what does the path `.users[].email` ask for?
choices: The first user's email only
The email field of every item in the users list
A user whose name is email
The number of users
items.explanation: [] iterates over every element of the users array, and .email picks the email field from each one, giving you one email per user. To get just the first you would write .users[0].email.

items.prompt: Which tool: a command prints structured JSON and you need one field's value out of it. Best choice?
choices: awk (split on spaces)
jq (query JSON by path)
sort (order lines)
tr (swap characters)
items.explanation: jq is purpose-built for JSON and reads it by path, so it cleanly extracts the field. awk splitting on spaces would break on the braces, quotes, and nesting of JSON.

lessons.title: What a Script Is: Shebang and chmod +x
lessons.content:
You already know how to type commands into the shell one at a time. But some jobs need the *same* handful of commands every time — backing up a folder, setting up a project, cleaning out old files. Retyping them is slow and easy to get wrong.

A **bash script** is the fix. A script is simply a plain text file that holds a list of shell commands. When you run the file, bash reads it top to bottom and runs each command in order, exactly as if you had typed them yourself. Think of it as **writing down a recipe** so you (or anyone) can repeat the steps perfectly, or a **macro** that replays your keystrokes.

Here is a complete tiny script:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "starting backup"
cp -r ~/work /backup
echo "done"
```

The first line, `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, is called the **shebang** (from "hash-bang", the `#!` characters). It tells the system *which program should run this file* — here, bash. Writing `#!/usr/bin/env bash` lets the system find bash wherever it is installed, which is the portable, works-everywhere form. The shebang must be the very first line.

Save that as `backup.sh`. One more step: a fresh text file is not yet allowed to run as a program. You give it permission once with **chmod +x** ("change mode, add e**x**ecutable"):

```bash
chmod +x backup.sh
./backup.sh
```

The `./` means "the file right here in this folder". From now on, `./backup.sh` replays all three commands.

**Recap:** a script is saved commands in a text file. Start it with the shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, make it runnable once with `chmod +x`, then run it with `./name.sh`.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a bash script?
choices: A special compiled program only experts can write
A plain text file containing shell commands that bash runs in order
A setting that changes how the terminal looks
A folder that stores your command history
items.explanation: A script is just a text file listing commands; running it is the same as typing those commands yourself, one after another. It is not compiled and it is not a terminal setting.

items.prompt: What is the main reason to put commands in a script instead of typing them each time?
choices: Scripts run faster than typing the same commands
So you can repeat a task exactly without retyping it, and share it
Typing commands by hand is not allowed on Linux
Scripts use less disk space than commands
items.explanation: A script captures a repeatable recipe: run it any time to redo the steps perfectly, and hand it to someone else. The commands themselves run at the same speed whether typed or scripted.

items.prompt: What does the shebang line `#!/usr/bin/env bash` do?
choices: It prints a welcome message when the script starts
It tells the system which program (bash) should run this file
It is a comment that bash ignores completely
It makes the file executable, replacing chmod
items.explanation: The shebang names the interpreter for the file, so the system knows to run it with bash. It does not print anything and does not set the executable permission — that is chmod's job.

items.prompt: Where must the shebang line appear in a script?
choices: On the very first line
On the last line
Anywhere, as long as it is somewhere in the file
Only inside a comment block
items.explanation: The shebang only works as the first line of the file. If anything (even a blank line) comes before it, the system will not recognize it as a shebang.

items.prompt: Why do you run `chmod +x backup.sh` before running the script?
choices: To save the file to disk
To give the file permission to run as a program
To translate the script into another language
To delete the old version of the file
items.explanation: A new text file is not executable by default. `chmod +x` adds the 'executable' permission so the system will let you run it. You only need to do this once per file.

items.prompt: You made the script executable. How do you run a script named backup.sh that is in your current folder?
choices: run backup.sh
./backup.sh
open backup.sh
bash.exe backup.sh
items.explanation: `./backup.sh` runs the file in the current folder; the `./` means 'right here'. There is no `run` or `open` command for this, and `bash.exe` is a Windows-style name, not how you launch it on Linux.

items.prompt: In what order does bash run the lines of a script?
choices: Bottom to top
In a random order each time
Top to bottom, one line after another
All lines at the same time
items.explanation: Bash reads the script from the top and runs each command in turn before moving to the next. This predictable top-to-bottom order is what makes a script behave like a recipe.

items.prompt: Inside a script, what does a line that starts with `#` (other than the shebang) do?
choices: It runs as a special command
It is a comment — a note for humans that bash ignores
It causes an error
It repeats the previous line
items.explanation: A `#` begins a comment, so bash skips the rest of that line. Comments let you leave notes explaining what the script does. The shebang only has special meaning because of the `!` and its position on line 1.

items.prompt: Why is `#!/usr/bin/env bash` often preferred over hard-coding a fixed path to bash?
choices: It runs the script twice for safety
It lets the system find bash wherever it happens to be installed, so the script is more portable
It makes the script run without bash at all
It is required for comments to work
items.explanation: Using `env` looks bash up in the normal search path, so the script works even when bash lives in a different location on another machine. It does not run anything twice and bash is still required.

lessons.title: Variables and echo
lessons.content:
A script gets far more useful once it can *remember* things. A **variable** is a labelled box that holds a piece of text. You put a value in it once and reuse it by name, so you do not repeat yourself and you can change a value in one place.

You create a variable by writing `name=value`. The rule that trips up every beginner: **no spaces around the `=`**.

```bash
name="Sam"
greeting="Hello"
```

(`name = "Sam"` with spaces does NOT work — bash would think `name` is a command.)

To *use* the value later, put a `$` in front of the name. This is called **expansion**: bash replaces `$name` with the text inside the box.

The **echo** command simply prints text to the screen. Combine them:

```bash
name="Sam"
echo "Hello $name"        # prints: Hello Sam
```

Quotes matter here. **Double quotes** `"..."` still let `$name` turn into its value. **Single quotes** `'...'` are completely literal — they print the `$name` as-is:

```bash
echo "Hi $name"     # Hi Sam
echo 'Hi $name'     # Hi $name
```

A habit to start right now: always wrap a variable in double quotes, like `"$name"`. You will see exactly why in a later lesson (it protects values that contain spaces). You can also capture the output of a command into a variable with `$( )`:

```bash
today=$(date +%F)        # today holds something like 2026-06-19
echo "Report for $today"
```

**Recap:** set a variable with `name=value` (no spaces). Read it back as `$name`, ideally quoted as `"$name"`. `echo` prints text; double quotes expand variables, single quotes do not.

items.prompt: What is a variable in a bash script?
choices: A command that deletes files
A labelled place that holds a value you can reuse by name
A type of comment
Another word for the shebang
items.explanation: A variable is a named box for a value (usually text). You store something once and refer back to it by name, which keeps scripts tidy and easy to change.

items.prompt: Which line correctly sets a variable named city to the value London?
choices: city = "London"
city="London"
$city = London
set city London
items.explanation: Assignment is `name=value` with NO spaces around the `=`. `city = "London"` fails because bash reads `city` as a command. The `$` is only for reading the value back, not for setting it.

items.prompt: Why does `count = 5` (with spaces around =) fail in bash?
choices: Numbers cannot be stored in variables
Bash reads `count` as a command name and `= 5` as its arguments
The value must be in quotes
Variables must be all capitals
items.explanation: With spaces, bash treats the first word as a command to run. Assignment must be written tightly as `count=5`. (Variables can hold numbers, and capitals are not required.)

items.prompt: You set `fruit="apple"`. How do you use that value later?
choices: Write fruit
Write $fruit
Write &fruit
Write echo(fruit)
items.explanation: Put a `$` in front of the name to expand it: `$fruit` becomes `apple`. Writing just `fruit` is plain text, and `&` / function-call syntax are not how bash reads variables.

items.prompt: What does the `echo` command do?
choices: It deletes text from a file
It prints text to the screen (standard output)
It repeats the last command you ran
It records audio
items.explanation: `echo` writes its arguments to the screen. It is the simplest way to show a message or the value of a variable. It does not delete anything or replay commands.

items.prompt: Given `name="Sam"`, what does `echo "Hi $name"` print?
choices: Hi $name
Hi Sam
$name
Hi "Sam"
items.explanation: Inside double quotes, `$name` is expanded to its value, so the output is `Hi Sam`. Double quotes allow variable expansion while still grouping the text together.

items.prompt: Given `name="Sam"`, what does `echo 'Hi $name'` print (note the SINGLE quotes)?
choices: Hi Sam
Hi $name
Sam
An error
items.explanation: Single quotes are fully literal: nothing inside them is expanded, so `$name` is printed exactly as typed. Use double quotes when you want the variable replaced by its value.

items.prompt: What does `today=$(date +%F)` do?
choices: Sets today to the literal text 'date +%F'
Runs the date command and stores its output in the variable today
Prints the date to the screen
Creates a file called today
items.explanation: `$( )` runs the command inside and captures its output as text. So `today` holds the result of `date +%F` (a date like 2026-06-19). This is called command substitution.

items.prompt: Which is the recommended habit when using a variable's value?
choices: Always leave it bare, like $file
Wrap it in double quotes, like "$file"
Wrap it in single quotes, like '$file'
Write it in capitals, like $FILE
items.explanation: Double-quoting `"$file"` is the safe default — it protects values that contain spaces (covered fully in a later lesson). Single quotes would stop the value from expanding at all, and capitalization does not affect safety.

lessons.title: Making Decisions: if, [[ ]] Tests, and case
lessons.content:
So far a script runs every line no matter what. A real program needs to **make decisions**: do this *only if* something is true. That is what `if` is for.

The shape is `if` ... `then` ... `fi` (`fi` is `if` spelled backwards, marking the end):

```bash
if [[ -f report.txt ]]; then
    echo "found it"
else
    echo "missing"
fi
```

The part in `[[ ... ]]` is the **test** — a question that is either true or false. The `then` block runs when the test is true; the optional `else` block runs otherwise. Always use the modern `[[ ]]` form in bash; the older `[ ]` (also spelled `test`) works but is fussier about spaces and quoting, so prefer `[[ ]]`.

Common tests:

```bash
[[ -f path ]]       # true if path is a file that exists
[[ -d path ]]       # true if path is a directory
[[ -z "$x" ]]       # true if the string $x is empty
[[ "$a" == "$b" ]]  # true if the two strings are equal
```

Comparing **numbers** uses a different bracket, the arithmetic `(( ))`:

```bash
if (( count > 0 )); then echo "have $count"; fi
```

Use `(( ))` for math (`>`, `<`, `==` on numbers) and `[[ ]]` for files and strings. (A classic mix-up: inside `[[ ]]`, `>` compares text alphabetically, not by size.)

When you have many possible values, a long `if`/`elif` chain gets clumsy. **case** is cleaner for matching one value against several options:

```bash
case "$1" in
  start) echo "starting" ;;
  stop)  echo "stopping" ;;
  *)     echo "usage: start|stop" ;;
esac
```

Each branch ends with `;;`, and `*)` is the catch-all default.

**Recap:** `if [[ test ]]; then ... fi` runs code only when the test is true; add `else` for the other path. Use `(( ))` for number comparisons, and `case` to match one value against many options.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of an `if` statement in a script?
choices: To repeat a command many times
To run a block of code only when a test is true
To define a variable
To print text faster
items.explanation: `if` lets the script branch: the `then` block runs only when the test is true. Repeating commands is what loops do, which is a separate idea.

items.prompt: What marks the end of an `if` block in bash?
choices: end
fi
endif
items.explanation: Bash closes an `if` with `fi` ("if" reversed). It does not use `end`, curly braces, or `endif` for this. Similarly `case` ends with `esac`.

items.prompt: What does the test `[[ -f config.txt ]]` check?
choices: Whether config.txt is empty
Whether config.txt exists and is a regular file
Whether config.txt is a directory
Whether you can write to config.txt
items.explanation: `-f` is true when the path exists and is a regular file. `-d` would test for a directory, and `-z` tests whether a string is empty.

items.prompt: In `if [[ -d backups ]]; then echo yes; else echo no; fi`, when does it print `no`?
choices: Always
When `backups` is NOT an existing directory
Only when `backups` is a file
Never
items.explanation: The `else` block runs whenever the test is false — here, when `backups` is not an existing directory. If it is a directory, the `then` block prints `yes` instead.

items.prompt: Which test is true when the variable mode equals the text "on"?
choices: [[ $mode = = on ]]
(( mode == on ))
[[ mode equals on ]]
items.explanation: String comparison uses `==` (or `=`) inside `[[ ]]`, with the variable quoted: `[[ "$mode" == "on" ]]`. `(( ))` is for numbers, not text, and there is no `equals` keyword.

items.prompt: Which is the right way to check whether the number in `count` is greater than 0?
choices: [[ count > 0 ]]
(( count > 0 ))
if count > 0
[[ $count -bigger 0 ]]
items.explanation: `(( count > 0 ))` does a numeric comparison. Inside `[[ ]]`, `>` compares strings alphabetically, so `[[ 9 > 10 ]]` would be true — a classic bug. Use `(( ))` for math.

items.prompt: Why is `[[ ]]` preferred over the older `[ ]` for tests in a bash script?
choices: `[[ ]]` is the only one that works at all
`[[ ]]` is more forgiving — it handles unquoted variables and empty values more safely
`[ ]` only works for numbers
There is no difference
items.explanation: `[[ ]]` is a bash keyword that avoids many word-splitting and quoting pitfalls of the older `[ ]` command. `[ ]` still works (and is needed for strict POSIX sh), but `[[ ]]` is safer in bash.

items.prompt: When is `case` a better choice than a long `if`/`elif` chain?
choices: When you need to loop over files
When you are matching one value against several possible options
When you only have a single true/false test
When you want to do arithmetic
items.explanation: `case` cleanly dispatches one value (like a command word) to many branches, each ending in `;;`. For a single condition, a plain `if` is simpler; for math you use `(( ))`.

items.prompt: In a `case` statement, what does the `*)` branch do?
choices: It multiplies the value
It is the catch-all default that matches anything not listed above
It ends the whole script
It comments out the branch
items.explanation: `*)` matches any value that did not match an earlier pattern, so it acts as the default (often an error/usage message). It has nothing to do with multiplication.

lessons.title: Loops: for and while
lessons.content:
Computers are great at doing the same thing many times. A **loop** repeats a block of commands so you do not have to copy-paste it. Bash has two everyday loops: `for` and `while`.

A **for loop** runs its body once for each item in a list. The body sits between `do` and `done`:

```bash
for color in red green blue; do
    echo "$color"
done
```

On each pass, the variable (`color` here) is set to the next item. So this prints `red`, then `green`, then `blue`. The list can be words you type, a number range, or a glob of filenames:

```bash
for n in 1 2 3; do echo "$n"; done       # 1 2 3
for f in *.txt; do echo "$f"; done        # every .txt file in this folder
```

Using a glob like `*.txt` directly is the **safe** way to loop over files, because bash hands each real filename to the loop intact — even names with spaces.

A **while loop** repeats *as long as a test stays true*. It is the right tool when you do not know the count ahead of time:

```bash
count=1
while (( count <= 3 )); do
    echo "pass $count"
    (( count++ ))         # add 1 to count
done
```

If you forget to change the test (here, increasing `count`), the loop never ends — an **infinite loop**. Press Ctrl-C to stop a runaway loop.

A very common `while` pattern is reading a file one line at a time, which you will meet again later:

```bash
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "got: $line"
done < notes.txt
```

**Recap:** a `for` loop repeats once per item in a list (use `*.txt` to loop over files safely); a `while` loop repeats while a test is true (remember to change the test, or it runs forever).

items.prompt: What does a loop do in a script?
choices: Runs a block of commands repeatedly
Deletes a block of commands
Hides output from the screen
Defines a new variable
items.explanation: A loop repeats a block of commands so you avoid copy-pasting. `for` repeats per item in a list; `while` repeats while a condition holds.

items.prompt: How many times does the body run in `for x in a b c d; do echo "$x"; done`?
choices: Once
Three times
Four times
Forever
items.explanation: A `for` loop runs once per item in the list. The list `a b c d` has four items, so the body runs four times, printing each in turn.

items.prompt: Which keywords wrap the body of a bash loop?
choices: begin ... end
do ... done
then ... fi
items.explanation: Both `for` and `while` loops put their body between `do` and `done`. `then ... fi` belongs to `if`, and bash loops do not use `begin/end` or braces for this.

items.prompt: In `for color in red green blue; do echo "$color"; done`, what is `$color` during the second pass?
choices: red
green
blue
the whole list red green blue
items.explanation: Each pass sets the loop variable to the next item. Pass one is `red`, pass two is `green`, pass three is `blue`. The variable holds one item at a time, not the whole list.

items.prompt: What does `for f in *.txt; do ... done` loop over?
choices: The literal text '*.txt' once
Each file ending in .txt in the current folder
Every file on the whole system
Only the first .txt file
items.explanation: Bash expands the glob `*.txt` into the matching filenames in the current folder, and the loop runs once per file. This is the safe, recommended way to iterate over files.

items.prompt: A `while` loop keeps running...
choices: exactly ten times
as long as its test stays true
until you save the file
only once
items.explanation: A `while` loop checks its test before each pass and continues while the test is true. It is ideal when you do not know in advance how many passes you need.

items.prompt: What is an infinite loop, and how is it usually caused?
choices: A loop that runs zero times because the list is empty
A loop that never ends because its test never becomes false (e.g. you forgot to update the counter)
A loop that runs on another computer
A loop that prints nothing
items.explanation: If the condition never turns false — often because the counter is never increased — the loop runs forever. Press Ctrl-C to stop it. Make sure each pass moves toward ending the loop.

items.prompt: What does `while IFS= read -r line; do ...; done < notes.txt` do?
choices: Reads notes.txt one line at a time into the variable line
Deletes every line of notes.txt
Counts the words in notes.txt
Writes new lines into notes.txt
items.explanation: This is the standard pattern for reading a file line by line: each pass puts the next line into `line`. The `< notes.txt` feeds the file in as input. `read -r` keeps the line exactly as written.

items.prompt: A loop is running forever and flooding your screen. What stops it?
choices: Pressing Ctrl-C
Closing the lid
Typing 'stop' and Enter
Waiting; it will end on its own
items.explanation: Ctrl-C sends an interrupt that stops the running command. An infinite loop will not end on its own, and there is no magic `stop` word for it.

lessons.title: Reading Arguments: $1, $@, shift, getopts
lessons.content:
A script becomes a real reusable tool when you can feed it different inputs each time, instead of hard-coding values. The words you type *after* the script name are its **arguments**, and the script reads them through special variables.

If you run:

```bash
./greet.sh Sam Alex Jo
```

then inside the script:

- `$1` is the first argument (`Sam`), `$2` the second (`Alex`), `$3` the third (`Jo`).
- `$@` is *all* the arguments as a list — always write it quoted, `"$@"`, so each one stays separate even if it contains spaces.
- `$#` is the *count* of arguments (here, `3`). `$0` is the script's own name.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "first is $1"
echo "there are $# arguments"
for name in "$@"; do
    echo "hello $name"
done
```

It is good manners to check you actually got an argument before using it:

```bash
if [[ -z "$1" ]]; then
    echo "usage: greet.sh NAME" >&2
    exit 1
fi
```

**shift** drops `$1` and slides everything down: the old `$2` becomes the new `$1`. It is handy for processing arguments one at a time in a loop.

For proper *named options* like `-o output.txt` or `-v`, counting positions gets awkward. Bash provides **getopts** to parse single-letter flags cleanly:

```bash
while getopts "o:v" opt; do
  case "$opt" in
    o) out="$OPTARG" ;;   # the colon after o means it takes a value
    v) verbose=1 ;;
    *) echo "usage: ..." >&2; exit 2 ;;
  esac
done
```

**Recap:** arguments after the script name arrive as `$1`, `$2`, ...; `"$@"` is the whole list and `$#` is the count. Use `shift` to step through them, and `getopts` for named `-x` style options.

items.prompt: What are a script's 'arguments'?
choices: Error messages it prints
The words you type after the script name when you run it
Comments inside the script
The script's variables
items.explanation: Arguments are the extra words supplied on the command line after the script name, like the file or name you want it to act on. They let one script handle different inputs.

items.prompt: You run `./backup.sh photos`. Inside the script, what is `$1`?
choices: backup.sh
photos
the number 1
empty
items.explanation: `$1` is the first argument after the script name, which is `photos`. The script's own name is `$0`, not `$1`.

items.prompt: Which variable holds ALL the arguments as a list?
items.explanation: `$@` is the full list of arguments (best written quoted as `"$@"`). `$#` is just the count, `$0` is the script name, and `$?` is the exit status of the last command.

items.prompt: You run `./tool.sh a b c`. What is the value of `$#`?
choices: a b c
items.explanation: `$#` is the number of arguments passed, which is 3 here. To see the arguments themselves you would use `"$@"`.

items.prompt: Why should you usually write `"$@"` with quotes when looping over arguments?
choices: Quotes make it run faster
So each argument stays as one piece, even if it contains spaces
Quotes are required or bash crashes
It changes $@ into a number
items.explanation: Quoted `"$@"` keeps every argument intact as a separate word, so an argument like `my file.txt` is not split on its space. Unquoted, it would break apart — the same quoting lesson as for any variable.

items.prompt: What does the `shift` command do to a script's arguments?
choices: Sorts the arguments alphabetically
Drops $1 so that the old $2 becomes the new $1
Reverses the order of all arguments
Adds a new argument at the end
items.explanation: `shift` removes the first argument and slides the rest down by one position. It is useful for processing arguments one at a time in a loop.

items.prompt: Why check `if [[ -z "$1" ]]; then ...; exit 1; fi` near the top of a script?
choices: To make the script run twice
To stop with a helpful message when the required argument is missing
To delete the first argument
To convert $1 to a number
items.explanation: `-z "$1"` is true when the first argument is empty (not supplied). Catching that lets you print a usage message and exit instead of failing in a confusing way later.

items.prompt: When is `getopts` the right tool?
choices: When you need to parse named single-letter options like -o and -v
When you want to loop over files
When you need to do arithmetic
When you want to print text
items.explanation: `getopts` cleanly parses short options such as `-v` or `-o value`, rather than you counting positional arguments by hand. For plain inputs, `$1`/`$@` are enough.

items.prompt: In a getopts string like `"o:v"`, what does the colon after `o` mean?
choices: The -o option is required
The -o option takes a value (an argument), available in $OPTARG
The -o option is disabled
It marks the end of the options
items.explanation: A trailing colon means that option expects a value; getopts puts that value in `$OPTARG`. So `o:` means `-o something`, while `v` (no colon) is a simple on/off flag.

lessons.title: Robust Scripts: Quoting, set -euo pipefail, Functions, Cleanup, and When to Use Python
lessons.content:
A script that works on your machine today should not quietly break tomorrow. This lesson collects the habits that make scripts trustworthy.

**Always quote your variables.** This is the single most common bash bug. If a filename has a space, an unquoted variable splits into two words:

```bash
file="my report.txt"
rm $file        # WRONG: bash sees rm "my" "report.txt" — two names!
rm "$file"      # RIGHT: stays one name
```

The same trap is why `for f in $(ls)` is buggy: the output of `ls` is split on spaces, so a name like `my report.txt` becomes two items. Loop with a glob (`for f in *`) and quote `"$f"` instead.

**Fail fast with `set -euo pipefail`.** Put this right under the shebang:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
```

- `-e`: stop on the first command that fails, instead of charging on.
- `-u`: error if you use a variable you never set (catches typos like `$flie`).
- `-o pipefail`: a pipeline fails if *any* stage fails, not just the last.

**Exit codes** are how programs report success: `0` means success, any other number means an error. Your script returns one with `exit 0` (good) or `exit 1` (problem); the special variable `$?` holds the last command's code.

**Functions** group commands under a name so you can reuse them. Mark a function's own variables `local` so they do not leak out:

```bash
greet() {
    local who="$1"
    echo "hello $who"
}
greet Sam
```

**Clean up with `trap`.** A `trap` runs a command when the script exits — even if it fails partway — so temporary files always get removed:

```bash
tmp=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$tmp"' EXIT
```

**Debugging:** run `bash -x script.sh` to see each command as it executes, and run **ShellCheck** (`shellcheck script.sh`), a free tool that flags quoting and other common mistakes before they bite.

**When to switch to Python.** Bash is perfect for short scripts that glue commands together. But once a script grows past roughly 100 lines, needs real data structures (lists, dictionaries, JSON), makes web/API calls, or you want to write tests, reach for **Python** (`subprocess`, `pathlib`, `argparse`) instead — it stays readable where bash gets painful.

**Recap:** quote every `"$var"`, start with `set -euo pipefail`, return clear exit codes, group reuse into `local` functions, clean up with `trap ... EXIT`, lint with ShellCheck — and move to Python when the job outgrows simple glue.

items.prompt: Why should you write `rm "$file"` instead of `rm $file`?
choices: Quotes make rm run faster
Without quotes, a filename containing a space splits into two separate names
rm requires quotes or it errors
Quotes delete the file more thoroughly
items.explanation: An unquoted `$file` is split on spaces, so `my report.txt` becomes two arguments (`my` and `report.txt`) and rm acts on the wrong things. Quoting keeps the value as a single piece. This is the most common bash bug.

items.prompt: Why is `for f in $(ls)` considered buggy?
choices: ls is not a real command
The output of ls is split on spaces, so filenames containing spaces break into multiple items
for loops cannot use ls
It only ever lists one file
items.explanation: The unquoted command output is word-split, so a name like `my report.txt` is treated as two entries. The safe pattern is `for f in *` (a glob), with the body using `"$f"`.

items.prompt: What does the `-e` in `set -euo pipefail` do?
choices: Echoes every command
Makes the script stop as soon as a command fails
Enables editing mode
Erases the screen
items.explanation: `-e` (errexit) halts the script on the first command that returns a failure, so it does not keep running after something has already gone wrong.

items.prompt: What problem does the `-u` option catch?
choices: Files that are too large
Using a variable that was never set (often a typo in its name)
Commands that run too slowly
Lines that are too long
items.explanation: `-u` (nounset) makes referencing an unset variable an error, so a misspelling like `$flie` is caught immediately instead of silently expanding to nothing.

items.prompt: By convention, what exit code does a program return to mean 'success'?
choices: Any number above 100
items.explanation: An exit code of 0 means success; any non-zero value signals an error. Your script reports this with `exit 0` or `exit 1`, and `$?` holds the last command's code.

items.prompt: Why declare a function's variables with `local`?
choices: It makes them run faster
So they stay inside the function and do not overwrite variables outside it
It is required or the function will not run
It prints the variable automatically
items.explanation: Bash variables are global by default, so an unmarked assignment inside a function can clobber a variable elsewhere. `local` keeps the function's variables private to it, avoiding surprising bugs.

items.prompt: What is the benefit of `trap 'rm -f "$tmp"' EXIT`?
choices: It deletes the script after running
It removes the temp file whenever the script exits, even if it fails partway
It traps and ignores all errors
It runs the script on every login
items.explanation: A `trap ... EXIT` runs your cleanup on every exit path — normal finish, error, or interruption — so a temporary file is always cleaned up. A plain `rm` at the bottom would be skipped if the script exited early.

items.prompt: How can you watch each command run, with its values filled in, to debug a script?
choices: shellcheck script.sh
bash -x script.sh
set -e
chmod +x script.sh
items.explanation: `bash -x script.sh` traces execution, printing each command after expansion just before it runs — great for seeing where things go wrong. ShellCheck is a separate, static check that does not run the script.

items.prompt: What is ShellCheck?
choices: A command that runs your script as root
A free tool that reads your script and warns about common mistakes like missing quotes
A way to encrypt scripts
A replacement for bash
items.explanation: ShellCheck statically analyzes a script and flags issues such as unquoted variables and other common bugs, before you ever run it. Running it and fixing its findings is a great habit.

items.prompt: Which situation is the strongest sign you should switch from bash to Python?
choices: The script uses a single pipe
The script needs to parse and manipulate nested JSON / data structures with real error handling
The script has one for loop
The script calls grep once
items.explanation: Nested data, JSON/HTTP, and real error handling are exactly where bash gets painful and Python (with json, pathlib, try/except, tests) shines. A single pipe, one loop, or calling grep are everyday bash and not reasons to switch.

items.prompt: What is bash best suited for, compared with Python?
choices: Large programs with complex data structures and unit tests
Short scripts that glue command-line tools and pipelines together
Building web servers
Heavy numerical computation
items.explanation: Bash excels at short glue: chaining commands, redirection, and file work. Once you need data structures, tests, or lots of logic (roughly past ~100 lines), Python is the clearer choice.

lessons.title: From Source Code to a Running Program
lessons.content:
You write a program as **source code** -- plain text in a language like C that humans can read. But a computer's processor cannot run that text directly. It only understands **machine code**, a stream of numbers that are its native instructions. So before your program can run, something has to translate the text you wrote into machine code. That translation is what this lesson is about.

Turning source into a runnable program normally takes **two steps**, not one.

Step 1 is **compiling**. The **compiler** is a program that reads one source file and produces an **object file** full of machine code. On Linux the usual C compiler is `gcc`. An object file (it ends in `.o`) is real machine code, but it is *not yet runnable* on its own -- it is just one finished piece.

Step 2 is **linking**. Most real programs are split across several source files, and they also use code written by other people -- for example the `printf` function that prints text. The **linker** takes all the object files, plus that shared **library** code, and joins them into one finished program you can actually run. Think of compiling as building each part of a car, and linking as bolting the parts together into a car that drives.

Here is the whole thing by hand for a program in two files:

```bash
gcc -c main.c        # compile main.c  -> main.o
gcc -c util.c        # compile util.c  -> util.o
gcc main.o util.o -o myprog   # link the .o files -> myprog
./myprog             # run it
```

The `-c` flag means "compile only, do not link yet". The last `gcc` line has no `-c`, so it links. The `-o myprog` names the output. For a quick one-file program, `gcc` will do both steps at once: `gcc hello.c -o hello`.

**Recap:** source code is human text; the processor needs machine code. **Compile** each source file into an object file, then **link** the object files and library code together into one runnable program. `gcc -c` compiles; `gcc` without `-c` links.

items.prompt: Why can't a processor run your C source file directly?
choices: Source files are too large to fit in memory
The processor only understands machine code, not human-readable text
Source files are encrypted for security
The processor can only run programs written in Python
items.explanation: Source code is text written for humans. A processor's hardware only executes machine code, its own native numeric instructions, so the text must first be translated. Size, encryption, and language choice are not the reason.

items.prompt: What does the compiler do?
choices: It joins several finished programs into one
It reads a source file and produces an object file of machine code
It runs the program and shows the output
It downloads libraries from the internet
items.explanation: Compiling translates one source file into an object file (machine code). Joining pieces together is the linker's job; running and downloading are separate activities entirely.

items.prompt: You ran `gcc -c main.c` and got main.o. Why can't you run main.o yet?
choices: An object file is one finished piece, not a complete linked program
Object files are always broken and must be deleted
main.o is still human-readable text, not machine code
You forgot to save the source file first
items.explanation: An .o file holds real machine code but is only a part. It still must be linked together with the other pieces and library code before it becomes a runnable program. (It is machine code, not text, so that option is wrong.)

items.prompt: What is the linker's job?
choices: To translate one source file into assembly
To join all the object files and needed library code into one runnable program
To check the source code for spelling mistakes
To split a program into separate object files
items.explanation: Linking is the step that combines the separate object files plus shared library code into a single finished, runnable program. Translating a single file is compiling; splitting is the opposite of what the linker does.

items.prompt: What does the `-c` flag in `gcc -c main.c` tell gcc to do?
choices: Compile only, and stop before linking (produce main.o)
Compile and link in one go, producing a runnable program
Clean up and delete all object files
Copy main.c to a backup file
items.explanation: `-c` means compile to an object file and stop -- it does not link. Running `gcc` on the .o files without `-c` performs the linking step. It has nothing to do with cleaning or copying.

items.prompt: Your program calls `printf`, which you did not write. How does that code end up in your finished program?
choices: The compiler types it out again from memory
The linker pulls printf's code from a shared library and includes it
printf is built into the processor hardware
You must paste printf's source into your own file
items.explanation: Common functions like printf live in libraries. At link time the linker finds that code and combines it into your program. The processor does not contain printf, and you do not need to paste its source in yourself.

items.prompt: What is the correct order of the two main steps to turn multiple source files into one program?
choices: Link each file, then compile them together
Compile each source file into an object file, then link the object files together
Run the program, then compile it
Download libraries, then delete the source files
items.explanation: First each source file is compiled into its own object file; then all the object files (and library code) are linked into a single program. Linking cannot happen before there are object files to link.

items.prompt: For a single-file program, what does `gcc hello.c -o hello` do?
choices: Only compiles, leaving hello.o that you must link by hand
Both compiles and links in one command, producing the runnable program hello
Only links, assuming hello.o already exists
Renames hello.c to hello without changing it
items.explanation: Without `-c`, gcc runs the whole pipeline: it compiles hello.c and links it, giving the runnable file named hello. It does not stop at an object file, and it does not merely rename the source.

items.prompt: People sometimes describe compiling as having an inner step where human-readable 'assembly' is turned into machine code by an 'assembler'. For a beginner, the safe summary is:
choices: Assembly and machine code are completely unrelated topics
The compiler ultimately produces machine code in an object file; the assembly stage is an internal detail gcc handles for you
You must always run the assembler by hand before gcc
Assembly is the name of the final runnable program
items.explanation: Internally a compiler often emits assembly, which an assembler converts to the machine code in the .o file -- but gcc does this automatically. You can treat 'compile = source to object machine code' as the working picture.

lessons.title: Build Systems: make, Makefiles, and CMake
lessons.content:
In the last lesson you compiled and linked a tiny program by typing `gcc` commands yourself. That is fine for two files. But a real project can have *hundreds* of source files. Typing all those commands by hand -- in the right order, every single time -- would be slow and full of mistakes. And if you change just one file, recompiling **all** of them is a huge waste of time.

A **build system** is a tool that automates this. You describe your project once; the tool then runs the right compile and link commands for you, and -- crucially -- it **only rebuilds the parts that changed**.

The classic build tool on Linux is **make**. You write a file called a **Makefile** that lists *rules*. Each rule has three parts: a **target** (the thing to build, like `myprog`), its **prerequisites** (the files it depends on), and the **recipe** (the commands that build it). Here is a small Makefile:

```make
myprog: main.o util.o
	gcc main.o util.o -o myprog

main.o: main.c
	gcc -c main.c

util.o: util.c
	gcc -c util.c
```

Now you just type `make`. The magic is how make decides what to do: it compares **timestamps**. If `util.c` is newer than `util.o`, make knows the object file is out of date and rebuilds it -- but it leaves `main.o` alone because `main.c` did not change. So editing one file recompiles one file, then re-links. It is like an assembly line that only re-does the one station whose part changed. (One famous gotcha: the recipe line must start with a real **Tab** character, not spaces.)

Hand-writing Makefiles gets hard as projects grow and need to work on Windows, Mac, and Linux. So most modern C and C++ projects use **CMake**, the de-facto standard today. CMake does **not** compile code itself. Instead you write a short `CMakeLists.txt`, and CMake *generates* the actual build files -- usually Makefiles or files for a fast tool called **Ninja** -- which then do the building:

```bash
cmake -S . -B build      # read CMakeLists.txt, generate build files
cmake --build build      # actually compile and link
```

**Meson** is a newer tool in the same spirit that also targets Ninja. The shared idea: you describe the project in a portable way, and the tool figures out the commands.

**Recap:** a build system runs your compile/link commands automatically and rebuilds only what changed. **make** reads a Makefile of targets, prerequisites, and recipes, using timestamps to skip unchanged files. **CMake** (and Meson) sit a level above: they generate the build files for make or Ninja, so one project description works everywhere.

items.prompt: What does a build system save you from?
choices: Writing any source code at all
Typing every compile and link command by hand, and needlessly rebuilding files that did not change
Needing a compiler like gcc
Having to name your files
items.explanation: A build system automates the compile/link commands and rebuilds only what changed, saving huge amounts of time on large projects. You still write the code and still need a compiler -- the build system just drives it.

items.prompt: You edit only util.c in a 100-file project and run `make`. What does make do?
choices: Recompiles all 100 files from scratch
Recompiles only util.c (and re-links), reusing the unchanged object files
Deletes every object file and refuses to build
Compiles util.c but does not re-link, so the program is unchanged
items.explanation: make sees that only util.c is newer than its object file, so it rebuilds just that one and then re-links the program. Reusing unchanged work is the whole point; it does not redo all 100 files.

items.prompt: How does make decide whether a file needs rebuilding?
choices: It always rebuilds everything to be safe
It compares timestamps: if a source file is newer than its object file, it rebuilds
It asks you to confirm each file
It rebuilds files in alphabetical order
items.explanation: make compares modification times. A source newer than its target means the target is stale and must be rebuilt; otherwise it is skipped. It does not blindly rebuild everything or go by file name.

items.prompt: In a Makefile rule, what are the three parts?
choices: Input, output, and error
Target (what to build), prerequisites (what it depends on), and recipe (the commands)
Header, body, and footer
Compile, link, and run
items.explanation: A rule names a target, lists its prerequisites, and gives the recipe (commands) to build the target from them. The other groupings are not how a Makefile rule is structured.

items.prompt: A Makefile fails with a 'missing separator' error. What is the most common cause for a beginner?
choices: The recipe line is indented with spaces instead of a real Tab character
The targets are listed in the wrong alphabetical order
There are too many comments
The file is larger than 1 kilobyte
items.explanation: make requires recipe lines to begin with a literal Tab. Editors that turn tabs into spaces cause the classic 'missing separator' error. Ordering, comments, and size are not the issue.

items.prompt: What is CMake's main job?
choices: It is a new compiler that replaces gcc
It reads a CMakeLists.txt and generates build files (Makefiles or Ninja files) that then do the building
It runs the finished program and times it
It is the part of the processor that executes machine code
items.explanation: CMake is a generator. From a portable CMakeLists.txt it writes the real build files for make or Ninja; it does not compile code itself. It is not a compiler, a runner, or hardware.

items.prompt: Why do large projects prefer CMake over a hand-written Makefile?
choices: CMake makes programs run faster once built
One CMakeLists.txt works portably across Linux, Mac, and Windows, instead of maintaining Makefiles by hand for each
CMake removes the need to compile code
Makefiles cannot build C++ at all
items.explanation: CMake lets you describe the build once and generate the right native build files on any platform, which scales far better than maintaining Makefiles by hand. It does not change runtime speed, and Makefiles can build C++ fine.

items.prompt: Both CMake and Meson can generate build files for which fast, low-level build tool?
choices: Ninja
gcc
bash
Python
items.explanation: Ninja is a minimal, fast build tool designed to be generated by higher-level tools like CMake and Meson. gcc is a compiler, bash is a shell, and Python is a language -- none is the build backend here.

items.prompt: Which statement is TRUE about how CMake and make relate?
choices: CMake and make are two names for the same program
CMake generates the build instructions; make (or Ninja) then carries them out by running the compiler
make generates CMakeLists.txt for you
Neither one ever runs the compiler
items.explanation: CMake sits above make: it writes the build files, and make or Ninja executes them, invoking gcc. They are different tools, make does not write CMakeLists.txt, and make certainly does run the compiler.

lessons.title: Libraries: Static (.a) vs Dynamic (.so) and ldd
lessons.content:
Programs reuse code. The functions for math, printing text, or compression are written once and shared by many programs. A bundle of such reusable code is a **library**. On Linux a library comes in two forms, and the difference is *when* the library's code joins your program.

A **static library** has a name ending in `.a`. When you link against it, the linker **copies the needed code straight into your program** at build time. The result is one self-contained file: it carries everything it needs, so it runs anywhere even if the library is not installed. The downside is the program is larger, and if a bug is found in that library, every program that baked it in must be rebuilt to get the fix.

A **dynamic** (or **shared**) library ends in `.so` ("shared object"). Its code is **not** copied in. Instead, your program just records *"I need libfoo.so"*, and the code is loaded from a single shared `.so` file on disk **when the program starts**. Many programs can share that one copy, so programs are smaller, and fixing a bug in the `.so` fixes every program at once. Picture it this way: a static library is a recipe printed inside each cookbook; a dynamic library is one shared cookbook on a shelf that many cooks open.

Because a dynamic library is found at *run time*, a special helper called the **dynamic linker** (also called the loader) runs first whenever you start a dynamically linked program. Its job is to find every `.so` the program needs and load it into memory. You can see what a program depends on with the **ldd** command:

```bash
ldd ./myprog
#   libfoo.so => /usr/lib/libfoo.so (0x00007f...)
#   libc.so.6 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6 (...)
```

This brings up a very common class of error: the program built fine, but when you run it you get something like `error while loading shared libraries: libfoo.so: cannot open shared object file`. That means the dynamic linker could not **find** the `.so` at run time. The library exists, but not in a place the loader looks. The quick (developer-only) fix is the environment variable **LD_LIBRARY_PATH**, which adds extra folders to the search:

```bash
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/path/to/libs
./myprog        # now the loader finds libfoo.so
```

For real software you install (not LD_LIBRARY_PATH) the library into a standard system folder so it is always found. The takeaway: with dynamic libraries, *building* successfully is not enough -- the library must also be **findable when the program runs**.

**Recap:** a static `.a` library is copied into your program at build time (self-contained but bigger). A dynamic `.so` library is loaded at run time by the dynamic linker and shared between programs (smaller, easy to patch). Use `ldd` to list a program's shared libraries; a 'cannot open shared object file' error means the loader could not find a needed `.so` at run time.

items.prompt: What is a library, in this context?
choices: A folder where you store your source files
A bundle of reusable code (functions) that many programs can share
The building where programmers work
A list of every program installed on the system
items.explanation: A library packages reusable functions -- math, text printing, compression -- so programs do not each rewrite them. It is not a source folder, a place, or an inventory of installed programs.

items.prompt: When does a STATIC library's (.a) code join your program?
choices: At build time -- the linker copies the needed code into the program
At run time, loaded fresh each launch
Never; static libraries cannot be used
Only when you run ldd on it
items.explanation: A static library is copied into the program by the linker at build time, making the program self-contained. Loading at run time describes a dynamic library, not a static one.

items.prompt: When does a DYNAMIC library's (.so) code get loaded?
choices: It is copied into the program at build time
At run time, when the program starts, by the dynamic linker
When you compile the source file
It is never loaded; it is only documentation
items.explanation: A .so is loaded at run time by the dynamic linker (loader) as the program starts. Its code is not copied in at build time -- that is the static model.

items.prompt: Which file extensions go with static and dynamic libraries on Linux?
choices: .a is static, .so is dynamic (shared)
.so is static, .a is dynamic
.lib is static, .dll is dynamic
.o is static, .c is dynamic
items.explanation: On Linux, `.a` (archive) is a static library and `.so` (shared object) is a dynamic one. `.lib`/`.dll` are the Windows names; `.o` is a single object file and `.c` is source.

items.prompt: A security bug is found in libfoo. Which linking style lets you fix every program that uses it by replacing one file, without rebuilding them?
choices: Static linking
Dynamic linking -- replace the shared libfoo.so
Neither; you must always rebuild every program
Both behave exactly the same
items.explanation: With dynamic linking the code lives in one shared .so loaded at run time, so replacing that file fixes all programs at once. Statically linked programs each baked in a copy and must be rebuilt.

items.prompt: What is an advantage of STATIC linking?
choices: The program is smaller than a dynamically linked one
The program is self-contained and runs even where the library is not installed
Many programs can share one copy in memory
A library bug fix automatically reaches the program
items.explanation: Because the code is baked in, a statically linked program needs nothing extra installed and runs anywhere. The other options (smaller size, sharing, automatic fixes) are advantages of dynamic linking.

items.prompt: What is the job of the dynamic linker (loader) when you start a dynamically linked program?
choices: To compile the program from source first
To find every .so the program needs and load it into memory before the program runs
To delete unused libraries from disk
To rename the program file
items.explanation: The dynamic linker runs at startup, locating and loading each required shared library so the program's calls into them work. It does not compile, delete, or rename anything.

items.prompt: What does `ldd ./myprog` show you?
choices: The source code of the program
The shared libraries the program needs and where each is found (or 'not found')
How long the program takes to run
A list of every user on the system
items.explanation: ldd lists a program's dynamic library dependencies and where the loader resolves each one, including 'not found' if it cannot be located. It does not show source, timing, or users.

items.prompt: Your program built fine, but running it prints: 'error while loading shared libraries: libfoo.so: cannot open shared object file'. What does this most likely mean?
choices: The source code has a syntax error
At run time the dynamic linker could not find the needed libfoo.so in any folder it searches
The program used too much memory
gcc is not installed
items.explanation: This is the classic 'library not found at run time' error: building succeeded, but the loader cannot locate the .so when launching. The fix is to put the library where the loader looks (or set LD_LIBRARY_PATH for development). It is not a compile or memory error.

items.prompt: What does setting LD_LIBRARY_PATH do, and how should you treat it?
choices: It permanently installs a library; use it for all production software
It adds extra folders for the loader to search at run time; it is handy for development but not the proper way to ship software
It compiles the library from source
It deletes the system library cache
items.explanation: LD_LIBRARY_PATH tells the dynamic linker extra directories to look in at run time -- useful while developing, but for real software you install the library into a standard location instead. It does not install, compile, or delete anything.

lessons.title: Cross-Compilation: Building for a Different Computer
lessons.content:
So far you built programs on your PC to run on your PC. But what if you want to write a program for a tiny **Raspberry Pi** or another small device? Those devices usually use a different *kind* of processor -- an **ARM** chip -- while your laptop most likely uses an **x86** chip. A program compiled into x86 machine code simply will not run on an ARM processor; the instructions are different, like two factories that speak different machine languages.

You could try compiling the program *on* the Pi itself, but small devices are slow and may not even have the tools installed. The better way is **cross-compilation**: you build the program on your fast PC, but produce machine code for the *other* kind of processor. It is like writing assembly instructions in another country's language so their factory can build your product.

Two words name the two sides. The **host** is the machine you build *on* (your PC). The **target** is the machine the finished program will *run on* (the ARM device). When host and target are the same kind of computer, that is ordinary ("native") compiling. When they differ, you are cross-compiling. (You will also hear the word **triplet** -- a short label like `aarch64-linux-gnu` that spells out the target's processor, operating system, and conventions. You do not need to memorize the fields; just know it names a specific kind of system.)

To cross-compile you need a special compiler that emits code for the target. A normal `gcc` makes x86 code; a **cross-compiler** makes ARM code. These are usually named after the target, for example `aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc` (aarch64 is 64-bit ARM):

```bash
sudo apt install gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu
aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc hello.c -o hello-arm
file hello-arm     # ELF ... ARM aarch64
```

The whole set of tools needed to build for a platform -- the **compiler**, the **linker**, and the **C library** (the standard functions every C program uses, like printf) -- is together called a **toolchain**. A cross-toolchain is just a toolchain that targets a different computer.

One more piece: if your program uses libraries, the cross-compiler needs the *target's* versions of those libraries and their headers, not your PC's. That collection is called a **sysroot** -- think of it as a copy of the target system's important folders, kept on your PC so the cross-compiler can link against the right files.

Finally, how do you test the ARM program if you have no Pi handy? Your x86 PC cannot run ARM code directly. The trick is **QEMU**, an emulator that *pretends* to be an ARM processor. In user-mode it can run a single foreign program:

```bash
qemu-aarch64 ./hello-arm   # run the ARM program on your x86 PC
```

A classic real-world snag: a program built for one **C library** may not run on a system that uses a different one. The two common C libraries are **glibc** (on most desktops) and **musl** (used by Alpine Linux). A glibc-built program on a musl system often fails at startup with a confusing 'No such file or directory' about its loader -- not because the file is missing, but because the matching C library is not there. The lesson: match the target's C library.

**Recap:** cross-compilation builds on your PC (the **host**) a program that runs on a different computer (the **target**, e.g. an ARM Pi), using a **cross-compiler** like `aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc`. The full tool set (compiler + linker + C library) is a **toolchain**; a **sysroot** supplies the target's libraries; **QEMU** can run the result on your PC; and a mismatched C library (glibc vs musl) is a common startup failure.

items.prompt: What is cross-compilation, and why would you do it?
choices: Compiling the same program twice on the same computer to be safe
Building a program on one kind of computer (your PC) to run on a different kind (e.g. an ARM device)
Mixing two programming languages in one file
Compiling a program over a network connection
items.explanation: Cross-compilation means producing machine code for a different processor than the one you build on -- for example, building on an x86 PC for an ARM Raspberry Pi, which is fast and convenient. It is not double-compiling, mixing languages, or networked building.

items.prompt: Why not just compile the program on the small ARM device itself?
choices: It is impossible to run a compiler on ARM
Small devices are often slow and may lack the build tools, so building on a fast PC is easier
ARM devices cannot store source code
Compiling on the device would erase the operating system
items.explanation: You can build on the device, but it is typically slow and may not have the toolchain installed, so cross-compiling on a powerful PC is the practical choice. The other claims are simply untrue.

items.prompt: Why won't a program compiled for x86 run on an ARM processor?
choices: ARM processors are always too slow
The two processors use different machine instructions, so x86 machine code is meaningless to an ARM chip
x86 programs are encrypted and ARM cannot decrypt them
ARM cannot read files created on x86
items.explanation: Different processor families have different instruction sets. Machine code built for x86 is not valid instructions for ARM, so it cannot run there. Speed, encryption, and file reading are not the reason.

items.prompt: You compile on your x86 laptop a program that will run on an ARM Raspberry Pi. Which is the host and which is the target?
choices: Host = the Pi (ARM), target = the laptop (x86)
Host = the laptop (x86), target = the Pi (ARM)
Host and target are both the laptop
Host and target are both the Pi
items.explanation: The host is where you build (the x86 laptop); the target is where the program runs (the ARM Pi). Because they differ, this is cross-compilation.

items.prompt: Which command cross-compiles hello.c for 64-bit ARM using a standard cross-compiler?
choices: gcc hello.c -o hello
aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc hello.c -o hello-arm
qemu-aarch64 hello.c -o hello
make hello.c arm
items.explanation: The cross-compiler is named after its target: `aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc` produces 64-bit ARM code. Plain `gcc` still makes x86 code; QEMU runs programs (it is not a compiler); and that make line is not valid.

items.prompt: What is a 'toolchain'?
choices: A single program that only links object files
The full set of tools to build for a platform: the compiler, the linker, and the C library
A chain of computers connected together
The list of files a program opens at run time
items.explanation: A toolchain is the compiler plus linker plus C library (and headers) that together turn source into a runnable program for a given platform. A cross-toolchain targets a different computer. It is not a single tool, hardware, or a file list.

items.prompt: What is a sysroot in cross-compilation?
choices: The root user account on the target device
A copy of the target system's libraries and headers, kept on your PC so the cross-compiler links against the right files
The folder where gcc is installed
A tool that emulates an ARM processor
items.explanation: A sysroot holds the target's libraries and headers so the cross-compiler links against the target's versions, not your PC's. It is not a user account, the compiler's location, or an emulator.

items.prompt: You cross-built an ARM program but have no ARM hardware. How can you run it on your x86 PC?
choices: Just double-click it; Linux converts ARM code automatically
Run it under QEMU user-mode, e.g. `qemu-aarch64 ./hello-arm`, which emulates an ARM processor
Re-link it with a flag to turn it into x86
Set LD_LIBRARY_PATH and it becomes native
items.explanation: QEMU user-mode emulation pretends to be an ARM processor and runs the foreign program on your x86 PC. The kernel will not run foreign machine code on its own, and no link flag or environment variable changes the instruction set.

items.prompt: A program built against glibc fails on an Alpine (musl) system with a 'No such file or directory' error, even though the program file is right there. The most likely cause is:
choices: The file permissions are wrong
A C-library mismatch: the system uses musl, but the program expects glibc's loader, which is absent
The disk is full
The program was built with optimizations turned on
items.explanation: The confusing message refers to the missing C-library loader the glibc program expects, not the program itself -- a classic glibc-vs-musl mismatch. Build against the target's C library (musl, here) to fix it. Permissions, disk space, and optimization are unrelated.

items.prompt: When is compiling called 'native' rather than 'cross'?
choices: When the host and target are the same kind of computer
When you use QEMU to run the result
Whenever you use CMake instead of make
Only when the program has no libraries
items.explanation: Native compiling means you build for the same kind of machine you build on (host = target). Cross-compiling is when they differ. The build system used, QEMU, and library use do not define native vs cross.

lessons.title: What Is a Container?
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Have you ever heard a programmer say "but it works on MY computer!" when an app breaks on someone else's machine? Containers were invented to make that complaint disappear.

**Build.** A **container** packages an app together with *everything it needs to run* -- the program itself, the code libraries it depends on, configuration files, even the right versions of those pieces -- into one neat, portable bundle. Think of a real **shipping container**: whatever you pack inside, the sealed box moves the same way onto a truck, a train, or a ship. A software container does the same for an app: because the bundle carries its own dependencies, it runs the *same way* on your laptop, a teammate's PC, or a server in the cloud.

Why does "works on my machine" happen at all? Usually one computer has a slightly different version of some library or setting than another, so the app behaves differently. A container freezes all of that *inside the bundle*, so the surroundings stop mattering. The popular tools for building and running containers are **Docker** and **Podman** -- we will meet both.

**Recap.** A container is a portable bundle of an app plus everything it needs, so it runs identically anywhere -- the cure for "it works on my machine."

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a container?
choices: A portable bundle of an app plus everything it needs to run
A faster type of hard drive
A programming language for the web
A backup copy of your files
items.explanation: A container packages the app together with its libraries, files, and the right versions, so the whole thing travels as one portable unit. It is not storage hardware or a language.

items.prompt: What problem were containers mainly designed to solve?
choices: Making screens brighter
The "it works on my machine" problem, where an app behaves differently on different computers
Typing faster
Reducing the price of laptops
items.explanation: By bundling the app with its dependencies, a container makes the surrounding machine stop mattering, so the app runs the same everywhere. The other options are unrelated to containers.

items.prompt: Why might an app run fine on one computer but break on another, without containers?
choices: The screens are different sizes
The machines may have different versions of libraries or settings the app depends on
One computer is newer
The keyboard layout differs
items.explanation: Apps depend on specific libraries and configuration; small differences between machines cause different behavior. A container locks those dependencies inside the bundle so they match everywhere.

items.prompt: The "shipping container" analogy fits software containers because a shipping container:
choices: Is always painted blue
Holds its contents in one sealed box that moves the same way anywhere
Can only carry food
Floats on water
items.explanation: Just as a sealed shipping container moves identically by truck, train, or ship, a software container carries its app and dependencies so it runs the same on any machine. Color and contents are beside the point.

items.prompt: Which of these does a container typically include in its bundle?
choices: Only the app's source code, nothing else
The app plus the libraries, files, and versions it needs to run
The entire internet
The user's personal photos
items.explanation: The whole point is to bundle the app together with its dependencies (libraries, files, correct versions). Shipping only the source code would leave the "works on my machine" problem unsolved.

items.prompt: Which two tools are the popular ones for building and running containers?
choices: Word and Excel
Docker and Podman
Chrome and Firefox
Photoshop and GIMP
items.explanation: Docker and Podman are the well-known container tools. The others are office, browser, or image-editing programs, not container engines.

items.prompt: If a container runs correctly on your laptop, what can you reasonably expect when you run the same container on a cloud server?
choices: It should behave the same way, because it carries its own dependencies
It will behave randomly
It must be completely rewritten first
It will only work at night
items.explanation: Carrying its dependencies is exactly what makes a container portable: the same bundle behaves the same way across machines. That predictability is the main benefit.

items.prompt: "Portable" in the context of containers most nearly means:
choices: Small enough to fit in a pocket
Able to run the same way across different computers
Battery powered
Easy to delete
items.explanation: Portability here means the container runs consistently on many different machines, not anything about physical size or power. That consistency is what teams rely on.

items.prompt: A teammate cannot get your app working after lots of setup steps. How does a container help?
choices: It emails them instructions
It bundles the app with its setup so they just run it -- no fragile manual steps
It makes their computer faster
It hides the error messages
items.explanation: Because the dependencies and setup are baked into the container, the teammate runs one bundle instead of reproducing many manual steps that are easy to get wrong.

lessons.title: Container vs Virtual Machine
lessons.content:
**Hook.** People often think a container is just a small virtual machine. That is the single most useful thing to get straight -- and it is wrong.

**Build.** Every computer has one **kernel**: the core part of the operating system that talks to the hardware (the CPU, memory, and disk). A **virtual machine (VM)** pretends to be a whole separate computer: it runs its *own* full operating system, with its *own* kernel, on top of the real machine. That is powerful but heavy -- like renting an entire separate kitchen just to make one sandwich. It takes lots of memory and can take a minute to "boot up."

A **container** is different. It does **not** bring its own kernel or its own operating system. Instead, all the containers on a machine **share the host's single kernel**, and the container only wraps up the app's own *userspace* -- its program and files. That makes a container much **lighter** and able to start almost **instantly**, because nothing has to boot. Compare it to a sealed **lunchbox** you carry into a kitchen that already exists, instead of renting a whole new kitchen. The trade-off: because they share one kernel, containers are isolated a little less strongly than VMs.

**Recap.** A VM carries a whole operating system and its own kernel (heavy, slow to start); a container shares the host's kernel and packages only the app (lightweight, instant). A container is *not* a VM.
