items.prompt: What is the KEY difference between a container and a virtual machine?
choices: A container shares the host's single kernel; a VM runs its own full operating system with its own kernel
A container is just a VM with a different name
A VM is always faster than a container
A container can only run on Apple computers
items.explanation: This is the central idea: a VM brings an entire OS plus its own kernel, while containers share the one host kernel and wrap only the app. They are not the same thing, and VMs are generally heavier, not faster to start.

items.prompt: What is a kernel?
choices: The core part of the operating system that talks to the hardware
A type of container image
A programming language
The screen of a computer
items.explanation: The kernel is the central piece of the OS managing CPU, memory, and devices. Containers share the host's one kernel rather than each carrying their own.

items.prompt: Why does a container usually start almost instantly while a VM can take much longer?
choices: Containers run on faster hardware
A container has no separate operating system to boot -- it just starts the app on the already-running host kernel
VMs are deliberately slowed down
Containers skip running the app
items.explanation: A VM must boot a whole guest OS and kernel; a container reuses the host kernel that is already running, so there is nothing to boot -- it simply launches the app.

items.prompt: The "sealed lunchbox vs renting a whole separate kitchen" analogy maps to:
choices: Lunchbox = VM, kitchen = container
Lunchbox = container (lightweight, uses the existing kitchen); separate kitchen = VM (a whole new setup)
Both mean the same thing
Neither relates to containers
items.explanation: A container is the lunchbox you bring into an existing kitchen (the shared kernel), while a VM is like renting an entire separate kitchen (its own full OS). That is why containers are lighter.

items.prompt: Roughly how many kernels run when you start ten containers on one Linux host?
choices: Ten -- one per container
One -- the host kernel, shared by all of them
Eleven
Zero
items.explanation: All containers share the single host kernel, so there is just one kernel no matter how many containers run. Ten separate kernels would describe ten VMs instead.

items.prompt: Compared with a virtual machine, a container is generally:
choices: Heavier and slower to start
Lighter and faster to start, because it shares the host kernel
Exactly the same weight
Unable to run real apps
items.explanation: Sharing the host kernel and skipping a full OS makes containers lighter on memory and near-instant to start, which is a major reason they are popular.

items.prompt: What does a container actually isolate, if not a whole operating system?
choices: Just the app's own userspace -- its program and files
The hardware itself
The electricity supply
Nothing at all
items.explanation: A container wraps the app's userspace (its process and files) while sharing the kernel. It does not virtualize hardware the way a VM does, and it does provide real isolation.

items.prompt: One trade-off of containers sharing a single kernel is that:
choices: They can never be deleted
Their isolation is a bit weaker than a VM's separate-OS isolation
They cannot use the network
They must run as a VM first
items.explanation: Because everything shares one kernel, the boundary between containers is thinner than between VMs, so isolation is somewhat weaker. This is the cost of being lightweight.

items.prompt: "A container is basically a lightweight virtual machine." This statement is:
choices: Correct in every way
A common misconception -- containers share the host kernel and don't run their own OS
True only on Windows
True only for Podman
items.explanation: It is the classic beginner misconception. The defining difference is that containers share the host kernel and package only the app, rather than running a full guest OS like a VM.

lessons.title: Images vs Containers (and Registries)
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A recipe is not the same as a cooked meal. Keeping that difference in mind makes containers click.

**Build.** An **image** is the saved, read-only package -- the *recipe* or the app *installer*. It sits on disk and does nothing by itself. A **container** is what you get when you *run* an image: the recipe actually being cooked, a live program doing work. The relationship is one-way and reusable: from a single image you can start **many** containers, just as one recipe can cook many identical meals.

Where do images come from? A **registry** is an online library of ready-made images. The most famous is **Docker Hub**. You **pull** (download) an image such as `nginx` (a popular web server) from a registry, then **run** it to create a container. Each image has a **tag** that picks a version, written `name:tag`, for example `nginx:1.27`.

```bash
docker pull nginx:1.27       # download the image from a registry
docker run nginx:1.27        # start a container from that image
docker ps                    # list the containers currently running
```

**Recap.** An image is the saved template (a recipe); a container is that image running (the meal). You pull images from a registry by `name:tag`, and one image can spawn many containers.

items.prompt: What is the difference between an image and a container?
choices: An image is the saved, read-only template; a container is that image actually running
They are two words for the same thing
An image runs; a container just sits on disk
A container is always smaller than an image
items.explanation: The image is the static package (the recipe); running it produces a container (the meal being cooked). The image does nothing on its own -- the container is the live process.

items.prompt: Using the cooking analogy, an image is like the ___ and a container is like the ___.
choices: meal ... recipe
recipe ... cooked meal
plate ... fork
oven ... kitchen
items.explanation: An image is the recipe (a saved set of instructions and ingredients); a container is the meal you cook from it. One recipe can make many meals, just as one image starts many containers.

items.prompt: What is a registry?
choices: A list of running containers on your machine
An online library you pull ready-made images from
A part of the kernel
A type of network cable
items.explanation: A registry (such as Docker Hub) stores images that anyone can pull. It is not a process list -- that is what `docker ps` shows -- nor part of the kernel.

items.prompt: In the image name `nginx:1.27`, what is the `1.27` part?
choices: The number of containers allowed
A tag that selects a specific version of the image
The price of the image
The amount of memory it uses
items.explanation: After the colon comes the tag, which picks a particular version of the image. It has nothing to do with container limits, price, or memory.

items.prompt: How many containers can you start from a single image?
choices: Exactly one, ever
Many -- one image can spawn many independent containers
Only as many as the image's version number
Zero; images cannot be run
items.explanation: Images are reusable templates, so one image can launch many separate containers, like cooking many meals from one recipe. They certainly can be run -- that is their purpose.

items.prompt: What does `docker pull nginx:1.27` do?
choices: Deletes the nginx image
Downloads the nginx image (version 1.27) from a registry
Starts ten containers
Edits the Dockerfile
items.explanation: `pull` downloads the named image and tag from a registry to your machine. Running it later (`docker run`) is the separate step that creates a container.

items.prompt: Why is an image described as "read-only"?
choices: You can only view it on a screen
It is a fixed template that isn't changed when you run it; the running container holds the changes
It cannot be downloaded
It deletes itself after use
items.explanation: The image stays unchanged as a stable template; any changes happen in the running container, not the image. That is what lets one image reliably produce many identical containers.

items.prompt: Which command lists the containers currently running?
choices: docker ps
docker delete
docker paint
docker sleep
items.explanation: `docker ps` shows running containers. The others are not real container-listing commands.

items.prompt: What is the usual order of steps to get a web server running from a registry?
choices: Run the container, then invent an image
Pull the image from the registry, then run it to start a container
Delete an image, then pull it
Start a VM, then a kitchen
items.explanation: First you pull (download) the image, then you run it to create a container. You cannot run an image you have not obtained, and you do not need a VM for this.

lessons.title: Using Containers: docker run, Ports & Volumes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You have an image. How do you actually start it, reach it from your web browser, and keep its data from vanishing? Three small ideas: run, ports, and volumes.

**Build.** The workhorse command is **`docker run`**. Give it an image name and it starts a container from that image. Handy flags: `-d` runs it in the background ("detached"), and `--name` gives it a friendly name.

A container is isolated, so by default the outside world cannot reach a server inside it. **Port mapping** opens a door. The flag `-p 8080:80` means "connect **host** port 8080 to **container** port 80" (the order is always *host:container*). Now visiting `localhost:8080` in your browser reaches the app listening on port 80 inside.

When a container is deleted, anything it wrote inside disappears. To keep data, use a **volume**: storage that lives *outside* the container and is mounted into it. The flag `-v mydata:/data` attaches a volume named `mydata` to the folder `/data` inside, so the files survive even if you delete and recreate the container.

```bash
docker run -d --name web -p 8080:80 -v mydata:/data nginx
docker logs web      # see the container's output
docker stop web      # stop it when finished
```

**Recap.** `docker run` starts a container; `-p host:container` maps a port so you can reach the app; `-v` attaches a volume so data outlives the container.

items.prompt: What does `docker run nginx` do?
choices: Deletes the nginx image
Starts a new container from the nginx image
Edits a Dockerfile
Lists running containers
items.explanation: `docker run` launches a container from the named image. Listing containers is `docker ps`, and running does not delete or edit anything.

items.prompt: In `docker run -p 8080:80 nginx`, what does `-p 8080:80` do?
choices: Maps host port 8080 to container port 80
Maps container port 8080 to host port 80
Opens ports 80 through 8080
Limits the container to 80 MB of memory
items.explanation: The format is host:container, so traffic to the host's port 8080 is forwarded to port 80 inside the container. It is not a range, and it has nothing to do with memory.

items.prompt: Why do you usually need port mapping to reach a web app inside a container?
choices: Containers cannot use networks at all
A container is isolated, so by default the outside world can't reach a server inside it
The browser is too slow otherwise
Ports cost extra money
items.explanation: Isolation means the container's ports aren't exposed by default; `-p` opens a door from a host port to a container port. Containers can use networks -- mapping just connects them to the host.

items.prompt: What is a volume used for?
choices: Making the container louder
Keeping data that should survive even if the container is deleted
Speeding up the CPU
Choosing the image version
items.explanation: A volume is storage outside the container, mounted in, so data persists across container deletion and recreation. Version selection is done with a tag, not a volume.

items.prompt: By default, what happens to files a container wrote inside itself when you delete that container?
choices: They are emailed to you
They disappear along with the container
They move to the registry
They become a new image automatically
items.explanation: Without a volume, in-container changes vanish when the container is removed. That is exactly why volumes exist -- to keep important data outside the container.

items.prompt: What does the `-d` flag in `docker run -d nginx` do?
choices: Deletes the container after one second
Runs the container in the background (detached)
Downloads a new image
Disables the network
items.explanation: `-d` runs the container detached, in the background, so your terminal is free. It does not delete the container or disable networking.

items.prompt: You ran `docker run -p 3000:80 nginx`. Which browser address reaches the app?
choices: localhost:80
localhost:3000
localhost:8080
It cannot be reached
items.explanation: The host side of the mapping is 3000, so you connect to localhost:3000, and traffic is forwarded to port 80 inside. The host number is the one you type in the browser.

items.prompt: In `-v mydata:/data`, what does `/data` refer to?
choices: A website address
The folder inside the container where the volume is attached
The host's password
The image's version
items.explanation: `/data` is the path inside the container where the named volume `mydata` is mounted, so writes there land in the persistent volume rather than the throwaway container.

items.prompt: Which command shows the output (logs) a running container has produced?
choices: docker logs
docker erase
docker paint
docker pull
items.explanation: `docker logs <name>` prints the container's output, which is the usual way to see what it is doing. `docker pull` downloads images; the others are not real commands.

lessons.title: Writing a Dockerfile: FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD & Layers
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Where do images come from in the first place? You write a short recipe file called a **Dockerfile**, and Docker bakes it into an image.

**Build.** A Dockerfile is a plain text file of instructions, read top to bottom. The main ones:

- **`FROM`** -- the starting point, a *base image* to build on (e.g. `FROM python:3.12` gives you Python already installed).
- **`COPY`** -- copy files from your project into the image (e.g. your code).
- **`RUN`** -- run a command while building, such as installing packages. Its result is baked into the image.
- **`CMD`** -- the default command to run when a container starts. (`ENTRYPOINT` is a close cousin that sets the fixed program to run; `CMD` often supplies its default arguments.)

Each instruction adds a **layer** -- a stacked slice of the image. Docker is smart: it **caches** layers and reuses any whose inputs have not changed (the **build cache**). So **order matters**: copy your dependency list and install packages *before* copying your source code. Then editing your code only rebuilds the small last layer, not the slow "install everything" layer.

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

Build it with `docker build -t myapp .` (the `-t` names the image; the `.` is the folder to build from).

**Recap.** A Dockerfile lists steps -- `FROM`, `COPY`, `RUN`, `CMD` -- and each step adds a cached layer. Put slow, rarely-changing steps first so the build cache saves you time.

items.prompt: What is a Dockerfile?
choices: A running container
A text file of instructions used to build an image
A registry of images
A type of volume
items.explanation: A Dockerfile is the recipe: a list of build steps Docker follows to bake an image. It is not the running container or the registry.

items.prompt: What does the `FROM` instruction do?
choices: Deletes the previous image
Sets the base image to build on top of
Runs the app
Maps a port
items.explanation: `FROM` chooses the starting base image (for example `python:3.12`), giving you a foundation to add to. Port mapping and running the app are separate concerns.

items.prompt: Which instruction copies files from your project into the image?
choices: COPY
FROM
CMD
STOP
items.explanation: `COPY` brings files (like your source code) from the build folder into the image. `FROM` sets the base, `CMD` sets the default run command, and `STOP` is not a Dockerfile instruction.

items.prompt: What does `RUN` do in a Dockerfile?
choices: Starts the container for users
Runs a command during the build (e.g. installing packages), baking the result into the image
Maps a volume
Pulls an image from a registry
items.explanation: `RUN` executes a command at build time and saves the result as a layer. The command that runs when a container later starts is set by `CMD`/`ENTRYPOINT`, not `RUN`.

items.prompt: What does `CMD` specify?
choices: The base image
The default command to run when a container starts
The registry URL
The host port
items.explanation: `CMD` sets the default command executed when the container launches (e.g. `python app.py`). The base image is `FROM`, and ports are handled at run time with `-p`.

items.prompt: What is a layer in a container image?
choices: A stacked slice added by an instruction, which Docker can cache and reuse
A physical disk
A type of network
The container's password
items.explanation: Each instruction adds a layer; Docker caches layers and reuses unchanged ones to speed up rebuilds. Layers are a software concept, not hardware or networking.

items.prompt: Why copy your dependency list and install packages BEFORE copying your source code?
choices: It looks neater
So editing your code doesn't bust the cache for the slow package-install layer
It is required or the build fails
To make the image larger
items.explanation: Docker reuses cached layers whose inputs are unchanged. If code is copied first, every code edit invalidates the later install layer; installing deps first keeps that expensive layer cached across code changes.

items.prompt: What does `docker build -t myapp .` do?
choices: Runs a container named myapp
Builds an image named myapp from the Dockerfile in the current folder
Deletes the myapp image
Pushes myapp to a registry
items.explanation: `docker build` reads the Dockerfile and produces an image; `-t myapp` names it and `.` is the build folder. Running, deleting, and pushing are different commands.

items.prompt: How is the relationship between ENTRYPOINT and CMD best summarized for a beginner?
choices: They do the exact same thing and cannot be combined
ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed program to run; CMD often supplies its default arguments
CMD sets the base image; ENTRYPOINT maps ports
Neither has anything to do with starting the container
items.explanation: A common pattern is ENTRYPOINT = the program that always runs, with CMD providing default arguments that a user can override at run time. Both relate to what runs when the container starts.

items.prompt: The "build cache" mainly helps by:
choices: Making containers run as root
Reusing unchanged layers so rebuilds are faster
Encrypting the image
Adding more ports automatically
items.explanation: The build cache reuses layers whose inputs haven't changed, so you don't redo slow steps each build. It is purely a speed feature, not about security or ports.

lessons.title: Under the Hood: Namespaces & cgroups
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A container felt almost magical -- a lightweight box around an app. The magic is really just two features built into the Linux kernel.

**Build.** Remember that a container shares the host kernel and is just an ordinary process. So how does that process get its own private "world" and stay within limits? Two kernel tools, working together.

**Namespaces** provide **isolation** -- they control what a process can *see*. Linux can give a process its own private view of several things: its own list of running processes, its own filesystem, its own network, its own hostname. Inside its namespaces, the app looks like it has the machine to itself, even though other containers are running right beside it. (Picture office cubicles: everyone shares one building, but each person sees only their own desk.)

**cgroups** ("control groups") provide **limits** -- they control how much a process can *use*. A cgroup can cap CPU time and set a memory ceiling, so one container cannot hog the whole machine. This is what enforces a flag like `docker run -m 256m` (limit to 256 MB of memory).

So the one-line truth: a container is just a normal process wrapped by **namespaces (what it can see)** and **cgroups (how much it can use)** -- all on the shared host kernel. No second operating system is involved.

**Recap.** Namespaces = isolation (a private view); cgroups = resource limits (a usage cap). Together they turn an ordinary process into a container.

items.prompt: What do namespaces provide for a container?
choices: Isolation -- a private view of things like processes, files, and the network
A memory limit
A faster CPU
An online image library
items.explanation: Namespaces isolate what a process can see, giving it its own view of processes, filesystem, network, and hostname. Capping resources is the job of cgroups, not namespaces.

items.prompt: What do cgroups (control groups) provide?
choices: A private network view
Resource limits -- caps on how much CPU and memory a process may use
A registry of images
The Dockerfile syntax
items.explanation: cgroups limit and account resource usage (CPU, memory, and more), so one container cannot starve the others. Providing a private view is what namespaces do instead.

items.prompt: The simplest summary of a container is:
choices: A small virtual machine with its own kernel
An ordinary process wrapped by namespaces (isolation) and cgroups (limits) on the shared host kernel
A backup file
A web browser
items.explanation: That one-liner captures it: a normal process plus namespaces for isolation and cgroups for limits, all on the host kernel. It is explicitly not a VM with its own kernel.

items.prompt: "Isolation" in a container most nearly means:
choices: The container is encrypted
The process gets its own private view, as if it had the machine to itself
The container runs without electricity
The app is deleted
items.explanation: Isolation here means the process sees its own processes, files, and network, unaware of its neighbors. That private view is exactly what namespaces create.

items.prompt: Which kernel feature enforces a memory cap like `docker run -m 256m`?
choices: Namespaces
cgroups
The registry
The Dockerfile
items.explanation: Resource limits are a cgroups feature: a cgroup sets the memory ceiling and the kernel enforces it. Namespaces only change what is visible, not how much can be used.

items.prompt: The "office cubicles in one building" analogy illustrates which idea?
choices: cgroups limiting memory
Namespaces giving each process its own private view while sharing one machine
Pulling images from a registry
Building layers in a Dockerfile
items.explanation: Everyone shares one building (the host kernel) but sees only their own desk -- that is namespace isolation. Limiting how much each person can use would be the cgroups idea.

items.prompt: Do namespaces and cgroups require the container to run a second operating system?
choices: Yes, each container boots its own OS
No -- they are features of the one shared host kernel; no second OS is involved
Only on weekends
Only for Podman
items.explanation: Both are built into the single host kernel that all containers share, which is why containers stay lightweight. Running a second OS would make it a VM, not a container.

items.prompt: If you wanted to stop one container from using all the host's CPU, which primitive handles that?
choices: Namespaces
cgroups
The registry
Port mapping
items.explanation: cgroups cap resource usage such as CPU time, preventing one container from monopolizing the machine. Namespaces and port mapping deal with visibility and networking, not usage caps.

items.prompt: Two containers run side by side. Why does each one seem to have its own separate set of running processes?
choices: Because each has its own kernel
Because a namespace gives each its own private process view
Because they take turns every second
Because cgroups hide the processes
items.explanation: A process (PID) namespace gives each container its own view of running processes. They still share one kernel, and it is namespaces -- not cgroups -- that control visibility.

lessons.title: Podman, Best Practices & a Glimpse of Kubernetes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Docker is the most famous container tool, but it is not the only one -- and a few simple habits make your containers much safer.

**Build.** **Podman** is an alternative to Docker that uses almost the same commands, so much that `alias docker=podman` often just works. The big difference is its design. Docker runs a single always-on background service called a **daemon** (`dockerd`) that runs as the powerful **root** user and owns every container. Podman is **daemonless**: there is no central background service -- it simply starts each container as a direct child of your command. This makes Podman naturally **rootless**: an ordinary user can run containers without root powers, and "root" *inside* the container maps to your ordinary unprivileged user *outside*. If a rootless container is broken into, the attacker only gets your limited account, not the whole machine.

```bash
podman run -d -p 8080:80 nginx     # same idea as docker, no daemon needed
```

A few **best practices** worth knowing:

- **Use a small base image** (like a slim or minimal image). Less software inside means fewer security holes and a smaller download.
- **Don't run as root inside the container.** Add a non-root user so a break-in does less damage.

**The horizon -- Kubernetes.** When you need to run many containers across many machines, automatically restarting and scaling them, teams use **Kubernetes** -- a system that orchestrates containers across a whole cluster. You do not need it to start out; just know it exists as the next step up.

**Recap.** Podman is a daemonless, rootless alternative to Docker with the same commands. Prefer small base images and a non-root user. Kubernetes is where containers go to scale across many machines.

items.prompt: What is the main architectural difference between Podman and Docker?
choices: Podman can only run one container
Podman is daemonless -- it starts each container directly, with no always-on root background service
Podman uses completely different commands you must relearn
Podman cannot map ports
items.explanation: Docker relies on a long-running root daemon; Podman skips it and launches containers as direct child processes. Its commands are intentionally Docker-compatible, and it supports ports normally.

items.prompt: What does "rootless" mean for a container?
choices: The container has no files
An ordinary, unprivileged user can run it; root inside maps to that limited user outside
It runs without any user account
It cannot access the network
items.explanation: Rootless means a normal user runs containers without host root powers; in-container root maps to their unprivileged account. It still has files and can use the network.

items.prompt: Why is rootless generally safer?
choices: It makes containers start faster
If a container is broken into, the attacker only gets the limited user account, not the whole machine
It encrypts every file automatically
It prevents all bugs
items.explanation: Without host root, a container escape yields only the unprivileged user's access rather than full control of the host. It is about limiting damage, not speed or magic bug prevention.

items.prompt: What is a daemon, in the Docker sense?
choices: A long-running background service that manages containers
A type of image
A network cable
A Dockerfile instruction
items.explanation: Docker's daemon (`dockerd`) is an always-on background service, running as root, that owns and manages containers. Podman avoids having one. It is not an image or a cable.

items.prompt: Why prefer a small (minimal) base image?
choices: It looks more professional
Less software inside means fewer security holes and a smaller download
It forces the container to run as root
It disables namespaces
items.explanation: A minimal base ships fewer packages, shrinking both the attack surface (fewer vulnerabilities) and the image size. It does not change the user or the kernel features used.

items.prompt: Why is running as a non-root user inside a container a best practice?
choices: Root cannot start programs
If the container is compromised, a non-root process limits the damage an attacker can do
Non-root containers run faster
It is required for the image to build
items.explanation: Least privilege: a compromised root process inside a container is far more dangerous than an unprivileged one, so adding a non-root user reduces risk. It is not about speed or a build requirement.

items.prompt: What is Kubernetes, in one line?
choices: A text editor for Dockerfiles
A system that runs and orchestrates many containers across many machines, restarting and scaling them
A replacement for the Linux kernel
A registry like Docker Hub
items.explanation: Kubernetes orchestrates containers across a cluster, handling scheduling, restarts, and scaling. It is not an editor, a kernel, or an image registry.

items.prompt: When would a team typically reach for Kubernetes?
choices: To start a single container on one laptop
When they need to run and scale many containers across many machines automatically
To write a Dockerfile
To pull one image from a registry
items.explanation: Kubernetes shines at scale -- many containers across many nodes with automatic restarts and scaling. For a single container on one machine, plain Docker or Podman is enough.

items.prompt: Roughly how different are everyday Podman commands from Docker commands?
choices: Totally different; nothing carries over
Very similar -- often `alias docker=podman` just works
Podman has no commands at all
Podman only accepts commands in binary
items.explanation: Podman deliberately mirrors Docker's command-line interface, so most commands are interchangeable and an alias commonly suffices. You do not have to relearn everything.

items.prompt: You start one small web service on a single server. Do you need Kubernetes?
choices: Yes, always
No -- plain Docker or Podman is plenty; Kubernetes is for scaling across many machines
Only if you use a volume
Only if you write a Dockerfile
items.explanation: A single service on one machine runs fine with Docker or Podman alone. Kubernetes adds value when you must orchestrate many containers across a cluster, which is overkill here.

lessons.title: From Empty Disk to Usable Storage: The Four Steps
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You plug a new hard drive into a Linux machine. Nothing happens — you can't save files to it yet. A fresh disk is just empty space, like a bare plot of land. Before you can use it, you do four small things in order. Once you see this chain, almost everything about Linux storage clicks into place.

**Build.** Think of building on an empty plot of land:

1. **Disk** — the raw, empty space (the bare plot). Linux names disks like `/dev/sda` (the first disk) or `/dev/nvme0n1` (an NVMe SSD).
2. **Partition** — you divide the disk into one or more sections, called *partitions*. Like surveying the plot into separate lots. A disk can have just one partition that fills it, or several.
3. **Filesystem** — you put a *filesystem* on a partition. A filesystem is the structure that organizes files and folders (the most common one on Linux is called **ext4**). Creating it is called **formatting**, and it wipes whatever was there. This is like building on a lot and setting up a filing system inside so you can find things.
4. **Mount** — finally you **mount** the filesystem: you attach it to a *folder* in Linux's single tree of directories (say, `/data`) so you can actually open and save files there. Like adding a driveway so you can reach the building.

Linux has one big directory tree starting at `/`. Mounting is how a separate disk shows up *inside* that tree at a folder of your choosing.

Two commands let you see all of this:

```bash
lsblk        # lists disks and their partitions as a little tree
df -h        # shows mounted filesystems and how full each one is
```

`lsblk` means "list block devices" (a *block device* is just the technical name for a disk or partition). `df` means "disk free." The `-h` makes sizes human-readable (like `20G` instead of a huge number).

**Recap.** Disk → partition → filesystem (format) → mount. Empty space, divided up, given structure, then attached into the tree so you can use it. The rest of this topic just zooms into each step.

items.prompt: Put the steps in order: you have a brand-new empty disk and want to store files on it. What is the correct sequence?
choices: Mount it, then format it, then partition it
Partition it, then format it (create a filesystem), then mount it
Format it, then mount it, then partition it
Mount it, then partition it, then format it
items.explanation: The chain is disk -> partition (divide into sections) -> filesystem/format (add the structure that organizes files) -> mount (attach it into the directory tree so you can use it). You cannot mount something that has no filesystem yet, and you cannot format a section that has not been partitioned, so the other orders are impossible.

items.prompt: What does it mean to "mount" a filesystem in Linux?
choices: To physically screw the disk into the computer case
To attach the filesystem to a folder in the directory tree so its files become reachable
To erase the disk and set up a fresh filesystem
To divide the disk into partitions
items.explanation: Mounting attaches a filesystem at a chosen folder (a mount point, e.g. /data) within Linux's single directory tree, so you can open and save files there. Screwing in hardware is physical installation, erasing is formatting, and dividing is partitioning -- different steps.

items.prompt: In the "empty plot of land" analogy, which everyday action matches FORMATTING a partition?
choices: Surveying the plot into separate lots
Building on a lot and setting up a filing system inside it
Adding a driveway so you can reach the building
Buying the bare, empty plot
items.explanation: Formatting creates the filesystem -- the structure that organizes files -- which is like building and adding an internal filing system. Surveying into lots is partitioning, the driveway is mounting, and the bare plot is the raw disk.

items.prompt: Which command lists the disks attached to the machine and the partitions on them as a small tree?
choices: `df`
`lsblk`
`cd`
`mount`
items.explanation: `lsblk` (list block devices) shows disks and their partitions in a tree. `df` shows free space on already-mounted filesystems, `cd` changes the current folder, and `mount` (with no arguments) lists what is currently mounted -- useful, but not the disk/partition tree.

items.prompt: A "filesystem" in this context is best described as which of the following?
choices: A brand of hard drive
The structure that organizes files and folders on a partition (for example, ext4)
The cable that connects a disk to the motherboard
The folder where Linux stores user accounts
items.explanation: A filesystem is the on-disk structure that turns raw space into named files and folders; ext4 is the common Linux example. It is not hardware, a cable, or a particular folder.

items.prompt: Why can't you just save files to a fresh disk the moment you plug it in?
choices: The disk needs to charge its battery first
It is raw empty space with no partition or filesystem yet, and it is not attached into the directory tree
Linux can only use disks made by certain manufacturers
You must reboot before any new disk works
items.explanation: A new disk is just empty space. It needs a partition, a filesystem (format), and a mount point before files can be stored. Disks have no battery, Linux is not brand-locked, and a reboot is not required to use new storage.

items.prompt: You run `df -h` and see a line for `/data` showing `20G` total and `5G` used. What does this tell you?
choices: The disk /data has not been formatted yet
A filesystem is mounted at /data, it holds about 20 GB, and roughly 5 GB are in use
There are 20 partitions on the disk
The /data folder cannot be written to
items.explanation: `df -h` lists mounted filesystems with their size and usage in human-readable units. A line for /data means something is mounted there; 20G total / 5G used is its capacity and current usage. It does not count partitions or imply the folder is read-only.

items.prompt: Linux organizes everything under a single directory tree starting at `/`. How does a separate second disk fit into that one tree?
choices: It gets its own separate tree with a different top, like D: on Windows
It is mounted at a folder (a mount point) inside the one tree, so its files appear under that folder
Its files are automatically copied into /home
It cannot be used until the first disk is removed
items.explanation: Unlike Windows drive letters, Linux has one unified tree. A second disk's filesystem is mounted at a folder (e.g. /data or /mnt/backup) and its contents appear there. Nothing is auto-copied, and multiple disks coexist fine.

items.prompt: What is the most important thing to remember about formatting (creating a filesystem on) a partition?
choices: It is reversible with a single undo command
It erases what was on that partition, so it is destructive
It makes the partition larger
It must be done every time you reboot
items.explanation: Formatting lays down a fresh, empty filesystem structure, which wipes existing contents -- it is destructive and not casually reversible. It does not change the partition's size and is a one-time step, not a per-boot one.

items.prompt: Match the analogy to the real step: "adding a driveway so you can reach the finished building" corresponds to which Linux step?
choices: Partitioning the disk
Mounting the filesystem
Formatting the partition
Buying the disk
items.explanation: The driveway is what finally lets you reach the building -- that is mounting, which makes the filesystem reachable in the directory tree. Partitioning divides the land, formatting builds and sets up the filing system, and buying the plot is acquiring the raw disk.

lessons.title: Disks & Partitioning: MBR vs GPT
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A partition divides one physical disk into separate sections, so you can keep things apart (for example, the operating system on one section and your data on another). To do that, the disk needs a tiny *map* near its front that records where each section begins and ends. That map is the **partition table**, and there are two kinds: the old **MBR** and the modern **GPT**.

**Build.** Think of the partition table as the index page of a notebook: it just lists where each chapter starts. Crucially, editing the map does **not** touch the data in the sections — it only changes the labels. (That is also why a corrupted map can make perfectly intact data suddenly unreadable.)

**MBR (Master Boot Record)** is the legacy scheme. It lives in the very first 512 bytes of the disk. Its limits: it only handles disks up to about **2 TiB**, allows only **4 main partitions**, and keeps a single copy of the map with no backup.

**GPT (GUID Partition Table)** is the modern default and the one to use unless you have a specific old-hardware reason. It supports **huge disks**, up to **128 partitions** by default, and keeps a **backup copy** of the map at the end of the disk in case the front gets damaged. (GUID just means a long, globally-unique ID number.)

The tools:

```bash
lsblk                  # SEE the disks and partitions (always look first)
sudo fdisk /dev/sdb    # interactive editor to create/change partitions
sudo parted /dev/sdb   # another partition editor, good for scripts
```

You will see partitions named by adding a number to the disk: `/dev/sda` has `/dev/sda1`, `/dev/sda2`. NVMe SSDs add a `p`: `/dev/nvme0n1` has `/dev/nvme0n1p1`.

**Recap.** A partition table is a small map at the front of the disk that carves it into sections. Prefer **GPT** (big disks, many partitions, a backup map) over the legacy **MBR**. Use `lsblk` to look before you touch anything with `fdisk` or `parted`.

items.prompt: What is a partition table?
choices: A list of every file stored on the disk
A small map near the front of the disk recording where each partition begins and ends
The part of the disk that holds the operating system
A table of users allowed to access the disk
items.explanation: The partition table is the disk's map of sections -- where each partition starts and stops. It is not a file list (the filesystem tracks those), not the OS itself, and not a permissions list.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly compares MBR and GPT?
choices: MBR supports huge disks and 128 partitions; GPT is limited to 2 TiB
GPT supports large disks, up to 128 partitions, and keeps a backup copy of the map; MBR is the older, more limited scheme
MBR keeps a backup map at both ends of the disk; GPT keeps none
GPT only works on disks smaller than MBR can handle
items.explanation: GPT is the modern scheme: big disks, up to 128 partitions, and a backup map at the disk's end. MBR is the legacy 512-byte scheme limited to ~2 TiB and 4 main partitions with a single, unbacked-up map.

items.prompt: For a brand-new modern machine with a large disk, which partition table should you normally choose?
choices: MBR, because it is older and therefore more reliable
GPT, the modern default that handles large disks and keeps a backup map
Neither; large disks do not need a partition table
Whichever one is alphabetically first
items.explanation: GPT is the recommended default on modern hardware: it handles large disks, allows many partitions, and stores a backup of the map. MBR's age does not make it more reliable, and any usable disk needs a partition table (or to be handed whole to another layer).

items.prompt: Why is it safe-by-design that editing a partition table does not move your file data?
choices: Because the table only records where sections start and end; it is just a map, not the data itself
Because Linux secretly copies all data to RAM first
Because partition editors refuse to run on disks with data
Because data is stored inside the partition table
items.explanation: The table is a small index of section boundaries; rewriting it changes labels, not the bytes in each section. (That is also why a wrong map can make intact data unreadable.) Data is not stored in the table, not parked in RAM, and editors will happily edit a disk that has data -- so be careful.

items.prompt: On a SATA disk named `/dev/sda`, how would its first and second partitions normally be named?
choices: /dev/sda-1 and /dev/sda-2
/dev/sda1 and /dev/sda2
/dev/part1 and /dev/part2
/dev/sdb and /dev/sdc
items.explanation: Partitions are the disk name with a number appended: /dev/sda1, /dev/sda2. NVMe drives insert a 'p' (nvme0n1p1). /dev/sdb and /dev/sdc would be separate disks, not partitions of sda.

items.prompt: You want to LOOK at the current disks and partitions before changing anything. Which command is the right first move?
choices: `sudo fdisk /dev/sda` and start deleting partitions
`lsblk`
`df -h /`
`rm -rf /dev/sda`
items.explanation: `lsblk` safely lists disks and partitions so you know exactly what you are working with. Jumping into fdisk to delete is dangerous, `df` shows mounted-filesystem usage (not the full partition layout), and `rm -rf` on a device path is a destructive mistake.

items.prompt: What is one practical risk that comes from MBR keeping only a single copy of its map in the first sector?
choices: The disk spins more slowly
If that one sector is damaged, the whole partition map can be lost with no backup to fall back on
It uses far more electricity
Files automatically shrink over time
items.explanation: MBR's single, unbacked-up map means damage to that first sector can lose the entire partition layout. GPT mitigates this with a backup copy at the disk's end. Disk speed, power use, and file size are unrelated to this.

items.prompt: Roughly what are MBR's two best-known limits?
choices: No limit on size, but only 2 partitions
About a 2 TiB maximum disk size and only 4 main partitions
Exactly 128 partitions and a 16 TiB cap
It cannot be read by Linux at all
items.explanation: MBR caps disks at roughly 2 TiB and allows only 4 main (primary) partitions. The 128-partition figure belongs to GPT, and Linux reads MBR just fine -- it is simply the older, more limited scheme.

items.prompt: Which pair of tools is used to create or change partitions (edit the map), as opposed to just viewing it?
choices: `df` and `du`
`fdisk` and `parted`
`cat` and `less`
`ping` and `ssh`
items.explanation: `fdisk` (interactive) and `parted` (also scriptable) edit partition tables. `df`/`du` report space usage, `cat`/`less` read text, and `ping`/`ssh` are networking tools.

items.prompt: What does the "GUID" in GPT (GUID Partition Table) refer to?
choices: A graphical user interface for disks
A long, globally-unique ID number used to label the table and its partitions
The brand name of the disk
A type of cable connector
items.explanation: GUID stands for Globally Unique Identifier -- a long unique ID. GPT uses these IDs to label the table and partitions. It is not a graphical interface, a brand, or a connector.

lessons.title: Filesystems: ext4, Journaling, mkfs & fsck
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A partition by itself is just a block of space — there is no notion of "a file" inside it yet. A **filesystem** is the bookkeeping that turns that space into named files and folders, and tracks where each file's data lives. On Linux the everyday default is **ext4**.

**Build.** Creating a filesystem on a partition is called **formatting**, done with the `mkfs` family ("make filesystem"):

```bash
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1     # format /dev/sdb1 as ext4 (this ERASES it)
```

The most important feature of ext4 is **journaling**. Imagine writing in pencil what you are *about* to do before you do it. If the power dies mid-write, the system reads that note on the next boot and either finishes the job or undoes it cleanly — so the filesystem stays consistent instead of becoming a corrupted mess. The journal protects the *structure*; it is a safety net against crashes.

A quick tour of other filesystems you'll hear about (you don't need them yet):

- **XFS** — another journaling filesystem, strong with very large files. It can grow but **never shrink**.
- **Btrfs / ZFS** — "copy-on-write" filesystems: instead of overwriting data, they write new copies, which makes near-free *snapshots* (instant point-in-time copies) and *checksums* that detect silently corrupted bytes ("bit-rot").
- **FAT / exFAT** — simple and understood by Windows, macOS, and cameras; good for USB sticks you share between systems.
- **tmpfs** — lives in RAM, so it is very fast but vanishes on reboot. Used for scratch areas like `/run`.

If a filesystem gets damaged (say, after a bad shutdown on a non-journaled disk), **fsck** ("filesystem check") repairs it:

```bash
sudo fsck /dev/sdb1     # only on an UNMOUNTED filesystem
```

**One firm rule:** never run `fsck` on a filesystem that is currently mounted and writable. The repair tool and the running system would have conflicting views and could corrupt your data. Unmount it first.

**Recap.** A filesystem (usually ext4) organizes a partition into files; `mkfs` creates one (and erases the partition). Journaling keeps things consistent across crashes. `fsck` repairs damage — but only when the filesystem is unmounted.

items.prompt: What is the job of a filesystem?
choices: To divide a disk into partitions
To organize a partition's space into named files and folders and track where each file's data is
To connect the computer to a network
To back up data to the cloud automatically
items.explanation: A filesystem turns raw space into files and folders and records where each file lives. Dividing into partitions is the partition table's job; networking and cloud backup are unrelated.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what does journaling (as in ext4) do?
choices: It keeps a diary of which users logged in
It notes what it is about to change before changing it, so a crash mid-write can be cleaned up and the filesystem stays consistent
It compresses every file to save space
It encrypts the filesystem
items.explanation: Journaling records the intended change first; after a crash the system replays or undoes it so the filesystem stays consistent. It is not a login log, not compression, and not encryption.

items.prompt: Which command creates an ext4 filesystem on the partition /dev/sdb1?
choices: `mount.ext4 /dev/sdb1`
`mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1`
`fdisk.ext4 /dev/sdb1`
`df.ext4 /dev/sdb1`
items.explanation: `mkfs.ext4` (make filesystem, ext4 variant) formats the partition. There is no mount.ext4/fdisk.ext4/df.ext4 in this sense -- mount attaches, fdisk partitions, and df reports usage.

items.prompt: A teammate is about to run `fsck` on the root filesystem while the system is running normally and the filesystem is mounted read-write. Why is this dangerous?
choices: fsck is always perfectly safe, so there is no danger
The running system is still changing the filesystem while fsck tries to repair it, so their conflicting views can corrupt the data
fsck will reformat the disk without asking
fsck only works on Windows filesystems
items.explanation: fsck assumes it has exclusive access. On a live, writable filesystem the kernel keeps changing things, so fsck's repairs are based on a stale picture and can corrupt data. Run it only when the filesystem is unmounted (or read-only). fsck repairs; it does not blindly reformat.

items.prompt: You need a filesystem on a USB stick that you will share between a Linux laptop, a Windows PC, and a digital camera. Which is the natural choice?
choices: ext4, because it is the Linux default
FAT or exFAT, because it is widely understood across operating systems and devices
tmpfs, because it is fast
XFS, because it handles large files
items.explanation: FAT/exFAT is the interop choice that Windows, macOS, Linux, and cameras all read. ext4 is Linux-centric, tmpfs lives only in RAM and disappears on reboot, and XFS is a Linux server filesystem -- none are ideal for cross-device sharing.

items.prompt: What is special about tmpfs, and what is the consequence?
choices: It is the most durable filesystem, ideal for long-term archives
It lives in RAM, so it is very fast but its contents disappear on reboot
It automatically encrypts everything
It can only be used on USB drives
items.explanation: tmpfs is RAM-backed: fast but volatile, so it is wiped on reboot -- great for scratch/temporary areas, wrong for anything you must keep. It is not durable, not an encryption layer, and not USB-only.

items.prompt: Copy-on-write filesystems like Btrfs and ZFS write new copies instead of overwriting data in place. Which two beginner-friendly benefits does that enable?
choices: Faster networking and lower power use
Near-instant snapshots (point-in-time copies) and checksums that detect silently corrupted data
Automatic partitioning and a backup partition map
Larger fonts and a graphical interface
items.explanation: Because old data is not overwritten, keeping a snapshot is cheap, and every new write can carry a checksum that flags bit-rot on read. These are storage-integrity features, not networking, partitioning, or UI features.

items.prompt: What must you always remember before running `fsck` on a filesystem?
choices: Make sure it is mounted and busy
Make sure it is unmounted (or mounted read-only) first
Make sure at least 10 users are logged in
Make sure it is the root filesystem
items.explanation: fsck needs exclusive, stable access, so the filesystem must be unmounted (or read-only) before checking. Checking a mounted, busy, writable filesystem -- especially the live root -- risks corruption.

items.prompt: What does formatting a partition (running mkfs on it) do to any data already there?
choices: It carefully preserves all existing files
It lays down a fresh, empty filesystem, effectively erasing what was there
It only adds new files and never removes any
It moves the files to another partition first
items.explanation: mkfs writes a new, empty filesystem structure, which wipes prior contents -- formatting is destructive. It does not preserve, merely-append, or relocate old data, so confirm the target first.

items.prompt: Which statement about XFS is correct?
choices: It can be shrunk easily at any time
It is a journaling filesystem that can grow but cannot be shrunk
It stores all data in RAM
It is only usable on Windows
items.explanation: XFS journals like ext4 and is strong with large files, but it can only grow, never shrink. It is a normal on-disk filesystem (not RAM-based) and is a Linux filesystem, not a Windows one.

lessons.title: Mounting & /etc/fstab: Making Mounts Stick at Boot
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Mounting by hand works for right now, but it is forgotten the moment you reboot. To make a filesystem mount **automatically every boot**, you add one line to a special file called **/etc/fstab** (the "filesystem table").

**Build.** First, the manual version. To attach a filesystem to a folder yourself:

```bash
sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /data     # attach /dev/sdb1 at the folder /data
sudo umount /data              # detach it again (note: umount, no 'n')
```

Now the automatic version. An `/etc/fstab` line has **six fields**, separated by spaces, in this order:

```
UUID=4d2a-9f1c   /data   ext4   defaults,noatime   0   2
```

1. **what to mount** — best given as a **UUID** (explained below), not `/dev/sdb1`.
2. **where** — the mount point folder, e.g. `/data`.
3. **type** — the filesystem, e.g. `ext4`.
4. **options** — how to mount it (see below).
5. **dump** — an old backup flag; use `0`.
6. **pass** — boot-time check order: `1` for the root filesystem, `2` for others, `0` to skip.

**Why a UUID instead of /dev/sdb1?** Disk names are not fixed. Add a disk or move a cable, and yesterday's `/dev/sdb` may become today's `/dev/sdc`. A **UUID** (Universally Unique Identifier) is a permanent ID stamped on the *filesystem itself*, so it always points to the right data. Find UUIDs with:

```bash
lsblk -f     # shows each filesystem's type and UUID
```

**Common options:** `defaults` (sensible normal settings), `noatime` (don't record a "last read" timestamp on every read — a small speed win), `ro` (read-only, protect the data), and `nofail` (don't halt the boot if this disk is missing — handy for removable drives).

**The boot caution.** A mistake in `/etc/fstab` can stop the machine from booting and drop you into an emergency repair prompt. So always test your new line *without* rebooting:

```bash
sudo mount -a     # mounts everything in fstab; surfaces typos right now
```

If `mount -a` succeeds with no errors, the line is safe.

**Recap.** Mount by hand with `mount`; make it permanent with a six-field line in `/etc/fstab`, keyed by **UUID** so it survives disk renaming. Always run `mount -a` to test before you trust it at boot.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of the /etc/fstab file?
choices: It lists every file on the system
It lists filesystems to mount automatically at boot, and where and how to mount each
It stores user passwords
It records network settings
items.explanation: /etc/fstab (filesystem table) tells the system what to mount at boot, at which folder, and with which options. It is not a per-file list, a password store, or network config.

items.prompt: Why should an /etc/fstab entry usually identify a filesystem by its UUID instead of by /dev/sdb1?
choices: UUIDs are shorter and easier to type
Disk names like /dev/sdb1 can change when hardware is added or moved, while a UUID is a permanent ID stamped on the filesystem itself
Linux cannot mount anything by /dev name
UUIDs make the disk faster
items.explanation: Kernel names (/dev/sdb1) depend on detection order and can shift, so an fstab keyed on them may mount the wrong disk or fail. A UUID names the filesystem itself and always follows the right data. UUIDs are longer, not shorter, and have no effect on speed.

items.prompt: What are the six fields of an /etc/fstab line, in order?
choices: mount point, what to mount, options, type, pass, dump
what to mount, mount point, filesystem type, options, dump, pass
type, options, what to mount, mount point, dump, pass
what to mount, options, type, mount point, pass, dump
items.explanation: The order is: what to mount (UUID/device), mount point, filesystem type, options, dump flag, pass (fsck order). The other orderings scramble these fields.

items.prompt: You added a new line to /etc/fstab and want to confirm it works WITHOUT risking a broken boot. What is the safe check?
choices: Reboot immediately and hope it comes back
Run `sudo mount -a`, which mounts everything in fstab and surfaces any typos right now
Delete /etc/fstab and start over
Run `fsck` on the live root filesystem
items.explanation: `mount -a` applies every fstab entry immediately, so a bad line shows up as an error while you can still fix it -- no reboot gamble. Rebooting blind, deleting fstab, or fscking the live root are all risky.

items.prompt: What is the real-world danger of a mistake in /etc/fstab?
choices: Nothing; fstab errors are silently ignored
A bad entry can stop the machine from booting normally and drop it into an emergency repair prompt
It will delete all your files
It changes your password
items.explanation: Because fstab is read during boot, a faulty line can halt boot and land you at an emergency shell. It does not silently vanish, delete files, or touch passwords -- which is exactly why you test with mount -a first.

items.prompt: Which command finds a filesystem's UUID so you can put it in /etc/fstab?
choices: `df -h`
`lsblk -f`
`du -sh`
`free -m`
items.explanation: `lsblk -f` lists each filesystem with its type and UUID. `df` shows usage of mounted filesystems, `du` measures folder sizes, and `free` reports memory -- none show UUIDs.

items.prompt: A removable backup drive is sometimes unplugged at boot. Which fstab option keeps a missing drive from blocking the whole boot?
choices: noatime
ro
nofail
defaults
items.explanation: `nofail` tells the system not to fail the boot if that device is absent. `noatime` skips access-time updates, `ro` mounts read-only, and `defaults` is the normal option set -- none of those address a missing device at boot.

items.prompt: What does the mount option `noatime` do, in beginner terms?
choices: It makes the filesystem read-only
It stops recording a "last read" timestamp on every file read, saving a little work
It mounts the filesystem only at night
It disables the filesystem's journal
items.explanation: `noatime` suppresses the access-time update that would otherwise happen on every read, trimming needless writes. It is unrelated to read-only mode, time of day, or journaling.

items.prompt: Which command detaches a currently mounted filesystem?
choices: `unmount /data`
`umount /data`
`mount -d /data`
`detach /data`
items.explanation: The command is `umount` (spelled without the first 'n'). `unmount`, `mount -d`, and `detach` are not the real commands -- a classic beginner spelling trap.

items.prompt: In the sixth fstab field (the "pass"/check order), what value is normally used for the root filesystem versus other data filesystems?
choices: 0 for root, 0 for others
1 for root, 2 for other filesystems (and 0 to skip the check)
9 for root, 9 for others
2 for root, 1 for others
items.explanation: The pass field sets boot-time check order: 1 for the root filesystem (checked first), 2 for other filesystems, and 0 to skip checking. Equal or reversed values are not the convention.

lessons.title: LVM: Pooling Disks & Resizing Storage on the Fly
lessons.content:
**Hook.** With plain partitions, a partition's size is locked in when you create it. If `/data` fills up, growing it is painful. **LVM** (Logical Volume Manager) fixes this by adding a flexible layer between your disks and your filesystems. Its superpower: you can **pool several disks together and resize storage while the system stays running** — no reboot, no repartitioning.

**Build.** Picture combining several plots of land into one big estate, then dividing that estate into rooms you can enlarge whenever you like. LVM has three layers, bottom to top:

1. **PV — Physical Volume.** A real disk or partition prepared for LVM with `pvcreate`. (Tagging a plot of land for the estate.)
2. **VG — Volume Group.** One or more PVs gathered into a single **pool** of space with `vgcreate`. (The combined estate.)
3. **LV — Logical Volume.** A virtual "disk" carved out of the pool with `lvcreate`. You put a filesystem on an LV and mount it, exactly like a partition. (A room in the estate.)

```bash
sudo pvcreate /dev/sdb /dev/sdc        # 1. prepare two disks as PVs
sudo vgcreate vg0 /dev/sdb /dev/sdc    # 2. pool them into VG 'vg0'
sudo lvcreate -L 20G -n lv_data vg0    # 3. carve a 20 GB LV named lv_data
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/vg0/lv_data        # format it like any partition
```

**Growing storage online — the headline trick.** Because the VG is a pool of free space, you can hand more of it to an LV and grow the filesystem while it stays mounted. The order matters: **enlarge the container first, then the filesystem.**

```bash
sudo lvextend -L +10G /dev/vg0/lv_data    # 1. make the LV 10 GB bigger
sudo resize2fs /dev/vg0/lv_data           # 2. grow the ext4 filesystem to fill it
```

**Snapshots.** LVM can take a **snapshot** — a frozen point-in-time view of an LV — which is great for taking a backup of a consistent moment while the system keeps running.

**Thin provisioning (one-line idea).** LVM can also hand out LVs that *promise* more space than physically exists, using real space only as data is written. Flexible, but you must watch the pool so it never actually runs out.

**Recap.** LVM stacks **PV → VG (pool) → LV**. Because the VG is a shared pool, you can grow an LV and its filesystem live with `lvextend` then `resize2fs` — the flexibility plain partitions don't have.

items.prompt: What does LVM let you do that plain, fixed partitions do not?
choices: Make the disk physically spin faster
Pool multiple disks into one space and resize storage (grow a volume) while the system stays running
Browse the internet without a network card
Run Windows programs directly
items.explanation: LVM's whole point is flexibility: pool disks into a volume group and grow logical volumes online, no repartitioning or reboot. Plain partitions are fixed at creation. It has nothing to do with spindle speed, networking, or running Windows software.

items.prompt: Match the LVM layers from bottom to top.
choices: LV (a disk prepared for LVM) -> VG (a virtual disk) -> PV (a pool)
PV (a disk/partition prepared for LVM) -> VG (a pool of PVs) -> LV (a virtual disk carved from the pool)
VG (a single disk) -> PV (a pool) -> LV (a partition table)
PV (a filesystem) -> LV (a pool) -> VG (a mount point)
items.explanation: Bottom to top: PV (physical volume = a disk/partition prepared for LVM), VG (volume group = a pool gathered from PVs), LV (logical volume = a virtual disk carved from the pool that you format and mount). The other orderings mislabel the layers.

items.prompt: Put the steps in order: starting from two raw disks, build an LVM logical volume you can format. What is the correct sequence of commands?
choices: lvcreate, then vgcreate, then pvcreate
pvcreate (prepare the disks), then vgcreate (pool them), then lvcreate (carve a logical volume)
vgcreate, then pvcreate, then mkfs
mkfs, then lvcreate, then pvcreate
items.explanation: You must prepare each disk as a PV (pvcreate), gather them into a pool (vgcreate), then carve an LV from the pool (lvcreate) -- only after that do you format with mkfs. The other orders try to build higher layers before the ones they depend on exist.

items.prompt: You must grow /data (an ext4 filesystem on /dev/vg0/lv_data) on a live system without downtime. What is the correct order?
choices: Run resize2fs first, then lvextend
Run lvextend to enlarge the LV, then resize2fs to grow the filesystem into the new space
Unmount it, reformat with mkfs, and remount
Run lvreduce, then mount -a
items.explanation: Enlarge the container first (lvextend), then grow the filesystem to fill it (resize2fs); ext4 grows while mounted. Running resize2fs first gives it no room to grow into, mkfs would erase the data, and lvreduce shrinks rather than grows.

items.prompt: What is the role of a volume group (VG) in LVM?
choices: It is the filesystem you mount
It is a single pool of space gathered from one or more physical volumes, from which logical volumes are carved
It is the partition table at the front of the disk
It is the folder where files are mounted
items.explanation: A VG pools the space of its PVs into one reservoir; LVs are then carved from that pool. It is not itself the mounted filesystem, the partition table, or the mount point folder.

items.prompt: Why does pooling disks into a volume group make online growth possible?
choices: Because the data is copied to a faster disk each time
Because the pool holds free space that can simply be assigned to a logical volume, updating a map rather than moving data, so it can happen while mounted
Because LVM turns off the filesystem during growth
Because the disks are physically merged into one platter
items.explanation: Growing an LV just assigns it more free space from the pool and updates LVM's internal map -- no data is relocated -- so it works on a live, mounted volume. Nothing is copied to a faster disk, the filesystem stays online, and disks are not physically merged.

items.prompt: What is an LVM snapshot useful for?
choices: Permanently deleting old files
Freezing a point-in-time view of a logical volume, for example to back up a consistent moment while the system keeps running
Speeding up the CPU
Encrypting the network connection
items.explanation: A snapshot captures an LV as it was at one instant, so a backup can read a consistent picture without stopping the system. It does not delete files, change CPU speed, or affect networking.

items.prompt: Which command prepares a raw disk or partition so it can be used by LVM (turns it into a physical volume)?
choices: `mkfs.ext4`
`pvcreate`
`mount`
`resize2fs`
items.explanation: `pvcreate` tags a disk/partition as an LVM physical volume. `mkfs.ext4` makes a filesystem, `mount` attaches one, and `resize2fs` grows an ext4 filesystem -- none of those create a PV.

items.prompt: LVM "thin provisioning" lets you create logical volumes that promise more space than physically exists, using real space only as data is written. What duty does that impose?
choices: None; thin pools can never run out
You must monitor the pool so it does not actually fill up, because writes fail if the real space runs out
You must reformat the pool every week
You must disable snapshots
items.explanation: Thin provisioning overcommits space, so the pool can genuinely run dry; if it does, writes to thin volumes fail. That makes monitoring pool usage mandatory. It does not require weekly reformatting or disabling snapshots.

items.prompt: After `lvextend` makes the logical volume bigger, why do you still need to run `resize2fs`?
choices: Because lvextend only enlarges the container; the filesystem inside must be told to expand to fill the new space
Because resize2fs re-encrypts the data
Because lvextend deletes the filesystem and resize2fs rebuilds it
Because resize2fs is what mounts the volume
items.explanation: lvextend grows the logical volume (the container), but the ext4 filesystem still thinks it is the old size until resize2fs expands it to fill the new room. resize2fs does not encrypt, delete/rebuild, or mount anything.

lessons.title: Swap, RAID, Encryption & Checking Your Storage
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A few more storage ideas round out the picture. You don't need to configure these as a beginner — the goal is to recognize the words and know roughly what each one is for.

**Build.**

**Swap — overflow space for memory.** When a machine runs low on RAM (its fast working memory), Linux can move some less-used data to a reserved area on disk called **swap**, freeing RAM for active work. Disk is far slower than RAM, so heavy swapping feels sluggish, but it stops the system from running out of memory entirely. Swap can be a partition or a file:

```bash
sudo mkswap /dev/sdb2     # prepare a swap area
sudo swapon /dev/sdb2     # start using it
```

A knob called `swappiness` (0–100) tunes how eagerly Linux reaches for swap.

**RAID — combining disks for safety or speed.** RAID groups several disks so they act as one, managed on Linux with a tool called `mdadm`. The common levels:

- **RAID 0** — splits data across disks for speed, but **no protection**: lose one disk, lose everything.
- **RAID 1** — a **mirror**: the same data on two disks, so one can fail and your data survives.
- **RAID 5** — spreads data plus recovery info across three or more disks; survives one disk failing while wasting less space than a full mirror.
- **RAID 10** — combines mirroring and striping for both speed and safety.

Key idea: RAID is about staying running when a disk dies — it is **not** a substitute for backups.

**Encryption (LUKS) — locking the data.** **LUKS** is Linux's standard way to encrypt a disk so the contents are unreadable without a passphrase — essential for laptops and removable drives that could be lost or stolen. It is set up with the `cryptsetup` tool; once unlocked with the passphrase, the disk is used normally.

**Checking your storage.** Three everyday commands answer "how full is it / what's using the space / what's the layout":

```bash
df -h        # free space on each mounted filesystem
du -sh *     # how much space each folder here is using
lsblk -f     # the disk -> partition -> filesystem map, with types and UUIDs
```

**Recap.** **Swap** is disk used as overflow memory; **RAID** combines disks for redundancy or speed (not a backup); **LUKS** encrypts a disk behind a passphrase. Day to day, `df`, `du`, and `lsblk -f` tell you how your storage is doing.

items.prompt: What is swap used for?
choices: Encrypting the disk
Disk space used as overflow when RAM (working memory) fills up, so the system does not run out of memory
Combining disks into one for speed
Storing the partition table
items.explanation: Swap is reserved disk space the system uses as overflow memory when RAM is full, trading speed for not running out of memory. It is not encryption, not disk-combining (that is RAID), and not the partition table.

items.prompt: Why does heavy swapping make a system feel slow?
choices: Because swap encrypts everything twice
Because disk is much slower than RAM, so moving working data to disk and back adds delay
Because swap disables the CPU
Because swap deletes files to make room
items.explanation: Swap lives on disk, which is far slower than RAM, so relying on it heavily introduces noticeable delay. It does not encrypt data, disable the CPU, or delete files.

items.prompt: What does RAID 1 (a mirror) give you?
choices: Maximum speed with no protection if a disk fails
The same data kept on two disks, so if one disk fails your data survives
More total storage than the disks combined
Automatic offsite backups
items.explanation: RAID 1 mirrors identical data onto two disks, so one can fail without data loss. The no-protection speed option is RAID 0; mirroring does not increase total capacity and is not an offsite backup.

items.prompt: Which statement about RAID 0 is correct?
choices: It mirrors data for safety
It splits data across disks for speed but offers no protection — if one disk fails, all data is lost
It survives two disks failing at once
It encrypts each disk
items.explanation: RAID 0 stripes data across disks for performance and capacity but has zero redundancy: losing any one disk loses everything. Mirroring for safety is RAID 1, and RAID 0 provides no fault tolerance or encryption.

items.prompt: A common and important misconception: is RAID a replacement for backups?
choices: Yes, RAID means you never need backups
No — RAID keeps you running if a disk dies, but it does not protect against deletion, corruption, or disaster, so you still need backups
Yes, but only RAID 5 replaces backups
No, because RAID actually erases data on a schedule
items.explanation: RAID guards against hardware failure of a disk, but an accidental delete, a corruption, or a fire affects all the disks together. Backups remain necessary. No RAID level replaces backups, and RAID does not erase data on a schedule.

items.prompt: What does LUKS provide?
choices: A way to combine disks for speed
Disk encryption, so the contents are unreadable without the correct passphrase
A faster filesystem
Automatic partitioning
items.explanation: LUKS is Linux's standard full-disk encryption: without the passphrase the data is unreadable -- ideal for laptops and removable drives. It is not disk-combining (RAID), a filesystem, or a partitioning tool.

items.prompt: You want to see how much free space is left on each mounted filesystem. Which command?
choices: `du -sh *`
`df -h`
`mkswap`
`cryptsetup`
items.explanation: `df -h` reports free and used space per mounted filesystem in human-readable units. `du` measures folder sizes, `mkswap` prepares swap, and `cryptsetup` manages LUKS encryption.

items.prompt: You need to find which folders in the current directory are eating the most space. Which command fits best?
choices: `df -h`
`du -sh *`
`swapon`
`lsblk`
items.explanation: `du -sh *` summarizes the size of each item in the current directory, pinpointing space hogs. `df` shows whole-filesystem usage (not per-folder), `swapon` enables swap, and `lsblk` lists block devices.

items.prompt: Which command shows the full disk -> partition -> filesystem map, including filesystem types and UUIDs?
choices: `free -m`
`lsblk -f`
`du -sh`
`mkswap`
items.explanation: `lsblk -f` lists block devices as a tree and adds filesystem type and UUID for each -- the one-stop layout view. `free` reports memory, `du` reports folder sizes, and `mkswap` prepares swap.

items.prompt: Which tool is used to manage software RAID arrays on Linux?
choices: `cryptsetup`
`mdadm`
`resize2fs`
`df`
items.explanation: `mdadm` creates and manages Linux software RAID arrays. `cryptsetup` handles LUKS encryption, `resize2fs` grows ext4 filesystems, and `df` reports filesystem usage.

items.prompt: Recognizing the words: a coworker says "the laptop's drive is LUKS-encrypted and we put the database on a RAID 1 mirror." What does that combination mean?
choices: The drive contents need a passphrase to read, and the database lives on two disks so one can fail without data loss
The drive is split for speed with no protection, and the database is stored in RAM
The drive is unencrypted, and the database has no redundancy
Both phrases describe swap space
items.explanation: LUKS-encrypted means the drive is unreadable without the passphrase; RAID 1 mirror means identical copies on two disks so a single disk failure does not lose the database. The other options confuse these with RAID 0, RAM storage, or swap.

lessons.title: Seeing Your Network: Your Address, Your Way Out, and a Reachability Test
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When your phone joins Wi-Fi, there's a settings screen showing your network name, your IP address, and the router. Linux has the same information, but you ask for it by typing commands. Three questions cover almost everything a beginner needs at first: *What is my address? Which way do I get out? Can I actually reach that other machine?*

**Build.** First, some plain words. A **network** is just machines that can talk to each other. An **IP address** is the number that identifies one machine on a network, the way a street address identifies one house (for example `192.168.1.42`). An **interface** is one network connection on your machine, usually a card or chip; common names are `eth0` (a wired connection) and `wlan0` (Wi-Fi). The **gateway** (also called the *default route*) is the device, normally your home router, that passes your traffic onward when you want to reach anything outside your own local network. Picture a building: your office has a room number (your IP), and the mailroom by the exit (the gateway) is where anything addressed to the outside world goes.

The one command for addresses and routes is `ip`. Ask it what your address is, and which way out it uses:

```bash
ip addr show     # your interfaces and their IP addresses (short form: ip a)
ip route show    # your routes, including the default gateway
```

In `ip addr`, the line starting `inet` shows your IP address. In `ip route`, the line starting `default via` shows your gateway. That's your address and your way out.

Now, *can you reach a machine?* The classic test is `ping`. It sends a tiny "are you there?" message and waits for the echo back, timing the round trip:

```bash
ping -c3 8.8.8.8     # send 3 probes to a host, then stop
```

If replies come back, the host is **reachable**. If nothing comes back, it is unreachable: it may be off, the address may be wrong, or something in between may be blocking you. The `-c3` just means "send three and stop" so it doesn't run forever.

One note for later: the modern command is `ip` (from a toolkit called *iproute2*). You may see an older command called `ifconfig` in old tutorials; it is outdated and often not even installed now. Reach for `ip`. The deep theory of *how* addresses and packets work (TCP/IP and the rest) lives in the separate Networking topic; here we just learn the Linux tools to see and control it.

**Recap.** `ip addr` shows your address, `ip route` shows your gateway (your way out), and `ping` tests whether you can reach another machine. Three commands, three everyday questions answered.

items.prompt: Which command shows your machine's own IP address?
choices: ip route show
ip addr show
ss -tulpn
items.explanation: `ip addr show` lists your interfaces and the IP addresses assigned to them (look for the line starting `inet`). `ip route show` tells you the way out (the gateway), `ping` tests reachability, and `ss` shows connections and listening ports -- none of those is where you read your own address.

items.prompt: What is the gateway (the "default route") on a typical home machine?
choices: The website you are currently visiting
Your own machine's IP address
The router that forwards your traffic toward anything outside your local network
The DNS server that turns names into numbers
items.explanation: The gateway is the device (usually your home router) that your machine hands off traffic to when the destination is not on your own local network -- like the mailroom by the building exit. It is not your own address, not the remote website, and not the name-lookup service (that is DNS, covered later).

items.prompt: You want to check whether a server is reachable from your machine. Which command is the simple first test?
choices: ping
ip link
ip addr
nft list ruleset
items.explanation: `ping` sends a small "are you there?" probe and waits for the echo reply, which is the quickest way to ask "can I reach this host?" `ip link` and `ip addr` describe your own machine, and `nft list ruleset` shows firewall rules -- none of those tests reachability to a remote host.

items.prompt: In the output of `ip addr show`, which part is your machine's IP address?
choices: The interface name, like eth0
